3: As the demand for poultry products increases (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2013), the poultry industry is expanding and consolidating (OECD/FAO, 2022), leading to an increase in poultry wastes such as manure. Poultry manure has major environ- mental impacts when it is applied to land, primarily from greenhouse gas emissions to air and emissions of nitrogen and phosphorus to surface water (Bolan et al., 2010). While not the largest source of agricultural emissions, land application of poultry manure contributes extensively to climate change impacts by increasing soil N2O emissions (Zhou et al., 2017), and the management of poultry manure in the U.S. in 2018 emitted 5.2 million metric tons of CO2-eq (United States Department of Agriculture, 2022). To ensure that progress is made toward limiting global temperature increases as set out in the Paris Agreement (IPCC,

2023), and make progress towards the 13th Sustainable Development Goal (SDG): Climate Action (United Nations, 2015) it is imperative to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from poultry manure management. Furthermore, land application of poultry manure can lead to extensive surface water impacts through eutrophication and contamination with harmful pathogens (Moore et al., 1995). While regulations such as the Clean Water Act in the U.S. exist to protect the environment, these regulations can be ineffective at reducing emission of nutrients to sur- face waters (Environmental Protection Agency, 2023). In New York state, poultry is the second largest producer of manure phosphorus, contributing to the overapplication of phosphorus near livestock oper- ations, especially with poultry operations in Sullivan county (Swink et al., 2009). To align with the 6th United Nations SDG: Clean Water and Sanitation (United Nations, 2015), it is necessary to reduce freshwater eutrophication impacts from poultry manure.
Due to these adverse impacts, extensive research has been conducted

AD|Anaerobic Digestion ALCA|Attributional Life Cycle Assessment AP|Aqueous Phase CAFO|Concentrated Animal Feeding Operation CHP|Combined Heat and Power CLCA|Consequential Life Cycle Assessment FE|Freshwater Eutrophication Potential FEWWN|Food-Energy-Water-Waste Nexus GP|Gaseous Phase GWP|Global Warming Potential HTC|Hydrothermal Carbonization HTL|Hydrothermal Liquefaction LCA|Life Cycle Assessment MILP|Mixed Integer Linear Programming MINLP|Mixed Integer Non-Linear Programming NPV|Net Present Value NYS|New York State SDG|Sustainable Development Goal TEA|Techno-Economic Analysis

on poultry manure's use as a feedstock for alternative resource recovery pathways, such as anaerobic digestion (AD) (Bolan et al., 2010; Bujoczek et al., 2000), slow pyrolysis (Baniasadi et al., 2016; Gunes et al., 2015), hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) (Ekpo 2016; Lu et al., 2018), and hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) (Hejna et al., 2022; Mau et al., 2019). In AD, microorganisms transform organic material under oxygen-free conditions to yield the renewable fuel biogas and digestate containing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium - necessary nutrients for crop growth (Liu and Rajagopal, 2019; Schulte et al., 2022). Pyrol- ysis converts biomass feedstock under temperatures of 300-600 °C with low oxygen contents, yielding renewable fuels syngas and bio-oil, an aqueous phase (AP), and the carbon sequestration agent biochar (Lehmann et al., 2021). HTL and HTC both can convert biomass in water at high pressures between 10 and 25 MPa, though HTL operates at temperatures of 280-370 °C (Toor et al., 2011) and HTC operates at temperatures of 180-220 °C (Funke and Ziegler, 2010). The primary yield of HTL is an energy-dense bio-oil (Akhtar and Amin, 2011); for HTC it is the coal replacement hydrochar (Wang et al., 2018). Both processes also yield a nitrogen-rich aqueous phase (Lu al., 2018) and a gaseous phase (GP) comprised primarily of carbon dioxide (Mau et al., 2016). By interconnecting these processes, such as using the aqueous phase of pyrolysis or hydrochar from HTC as a feedstock for AD, yields from biogas can increase (Fang et al., 2020; Torri and Fabbri, 2014), maximizing the quantity of sustainable fuels that can be produced. However, the high costs of these technologies, mitigated partially through the valorization of the end products, still presents barriers to widespread adoption (Patel et al., 2016).

To evaluate the environmental sustainability of these technologies for the conversion of poultry manure, several life cycle assessments (LCA) have been conducted. Attributional LCAs (ALCA) capture impacts assigned to a certain product or service, while consequential LCAs (CLCA) additionally quantify the environmental impacts from changes to other processes through marginal economic relationships (Earles and Halog, 2011). Both techniques have drawbacks, as the system boundary in an ALCA can be seen as arbitrary, and there is a significant data collection problem for the offset processes in a CLCA (Earles and Halog, 2011). Using an ALCA, it was found that the use of pyrolysis, gasifica- tion, HTL, and HTC decreased aquatic eutrophication by 69-99% and reduced the climate change impact by 15-53% compared to direct land application (Bora et al., 2020a). In a CLCA for AD of poultry litter in a single, fixed-size facility, the eutrophication impact was found to vary

based on the biogas use cases and that a reduction in climate change impact results from avoided electricity generation and avoided peat moss production (Beausang et al., 2020). AD of poultry litter was also found to reduce the climate impact compared to land application across multiple ALCA studies (Hassanein et al., 2022; Nusselder et al., 2020). Direct combustion of poultry manure to produce renewable energy was found to reduce global warming impact compared to direct land appli- cation through the avoidance of emissions of NH3, NO, and N2O from poultry manure (Billen et al., 2015), producing sustainable energy that contributes towards SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy (United Na- tions, 2015).

There remains a critical research gap to identify which set of tech- nologies minimizes key environmental impacts while maintaining eco- nomic feasibility. Moreover, there is a knowledge gap to understand the consequences of modifying the system boundary assumptions to avoid creating negative externalities in related processes by shifting the environmental burden outside the system boundary. To comprehen- sively assess the major environmental harms of direct land application of poultry manure and the potential clean energy and carbon sequestration benefits from alternative poultry manure management practices, poultry manure in incorporated into the food-energy-water-waste nexus (FEWWN). To create the FEWWN, wastes are added to the food-energy- water nexus (Garcia and You, 2016), which can be optimized to pursue maximum environmental and economic sustainability (Garcia et al., 2019; Kibler et al., 2018). Moreover, as alternative poultry manure management systems can produce valuable solid, liquid, and gaseous products, it is further necessary to consider the economic value of the converted products through a circular economy context (Geissdoerfer et al., 2017).

To address the research gaps in the context of the FEWWN and the circular economy, we introduce a multi-objective optimization frame- work that combines techno-economic assessment (TEA) and LCAs as depicted in Fig. 1. This framework is applied to a novel biomass pro- cessing problem with a focus on potential fertilizer application of solid products from poultry manure management pathways including direct land application, pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, and AD. This model is optimized according to three objective functions, maximizing the net present value, and minimizing the global warming potential (GWP) and the freshwater eutrophication potential (FE), using a weighted sums approach for facilities of different scales. This approach enables the discovery of economically sensible and environmentally sustainable management practices that align with SDGs 6, 7, and 13 and address the major environmental harms of land application of poultry manure. Evaluating this model using a novel spatial analysis for the state of New York (NYS) yields the first comparison of the life cycle type to under- stand the tradeoffs between maintaining food production, preserving water quality, and producing of renewable energy using poultry manure. It also reveals the additional impacts caused by the inclusion of markets such as upstream fertilizer markets and downstream electricity markets. A sensitivity analysis is conducted for the solutions that maximize the net present value (NPV), minimize the GWP, and a tradeoff solution to assess the economic effects of uncertainty in the implementation of optimal technologies.

Our results yield several policy implications, especially with regards to improving the economics of poultry manure management to accel- erate adoption of environmentally sustainable technologies. First, veri- fiable, high-integrity carbon offset credits should be issued by expanding carbon financing to increase the profitability of constructing pyrolysis plants to produce biochar from poultry manure and contribute towards SDG 13. For technologies like AD, innovations in environmentally sus- tainable materials for the construction of facilities should receive gov- ernment incentives to reduce the upstream and downstream environmental impacts for a technology with relatively low capital costs and low environmental impacts under an ALCA regime. As several technologies produce waste products, the upgrading of bio-oil, AP and GP into higher quality fuels or value-added products should be

Fig. 1. System boundaries and optimization framework. The life cycle optimization framework includes an attributional life cycle assessment (ALCA), a conse- quential life cycle assessment (CLCA), multi-objective optimization, and a spatial analysis case study in New York state. Material flows and relationships in the ALCA are given in dark grey arrows, and the system is bounded by the dark blue box. The CLCA is bounded by a red box, and all material flows in the ALCA are in the CLCA. The CLCA additionally contains relationships between upstream and downstream markets demarcated with purple arrows. Aligning with the multi-objective opti- mization criteria, major emissions to air are given by three light grey arrows and major emissions to water are given by three blue products by relevant system processes. Products in A3 include fertilizer, diesel, electricity, and natural gas, and are inputs to the thermochemical conversion facilities alongside poultry manure. In stage C, Facility choice as discussed in Section 1, Facility sizing as discussed in Section 2.2, alongside operating temperatures (for HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis) and loading rate (for AD) as further discussed in Section 2.1.2 are the optimization variables being considered. Products D1-D and End Uses E1-E4 are grouped for visual clarity - conversion technology-specific products and product-specific end uses are given in Section 2.1.2.

incentivized to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and increase the available revenues for poultry farms through an increased supply of renewable fuels, further integrating poultry manure management into the circular economy. Finally, innovations in the miniaturization and commercialization of pyrolysis reactors should receive government in- centives to reduce the economic barrier of the construction and adoption of farms possessing small quantities of poultry manure (~1-100 tons manure/month), which are at best on the order of US$200/ton manure. In the Materials and Methods section, the methodologies of the LCAs, TEA, multi-objective optimization, and spatial analysis are described. In the Results and Discussion section, the spatial analysis and a tradeoff analysis using a three-dimensional pareto front is conducted. This is followed by a sensitivity analysis.

HTL and HTC both can convert biomass in water at high pressures between 10 and 25 MPa, though HTL operates at temperatures of 280-370 °C (Toor et al., 2011) and HTC operates at temperatures of 180-220 °C (Funke and Ziegler, 2010). The primary yield of HTL is an energy-dense bio-oil (Akhtar and Amin, 2011); for HTC it is the coal replacement hydrochar (Wang et al., 2018). Both processes also yield a nitrogen-rich aqueous phase (Lu al., 2018) and a gaseous phase (GP) comprised primarily of carbon dioxide (Mau et al., 2016). By interconnecting these processes, such as using the aqueous phase of pyrolysis or hydrochar from HTC as a feedstock for AD, yields from biogas can increase (Fang et al., 2020; Torri and Fabbri, 2014), maximizing the quantity of sustainable fuels that can be produced. However, the high costs of these technologies, mitigated partially through the valorization of the end products, still presents barriers to widespread adoption (Patel et al., 2016).

To evaluate the environmental sustainability of these technologies for the conversion of poultry manure, several life cycle assessments (LCA) have been conducted. Attributional LCAs (ALCA) capture impacts assigned to a certain product or service, while consequential LCAs (CLCA) additionally quantify the environmental impacts from changes to other processes through marginal economic relationships (Earles and Halog, 2011). Both techniques have drawbacks, as the system boundary in an ALCA can be seen as arbitrary, and there is a significant data collection problem for the offset processes in a CLCA (Earles and Halog, 2011). Using an ALCA, it was found that the use of pyrolysis, gasifica- tion, HTL, and HTC decreased aquatic eutrophication by 69-99% and reduced the climate change impact by 15-53% compared to direct land application (Bora et al., 2020a). In a CLCA for AD of poultry litter in a single, fixed-size facility, the eutrophication impact was found to vary

based on the biogas use cases and that a reduction in climate change impact results from avoided electricity generation and avoided peat moss production (Beausang et al., 2020). AD of poultry litter was also found to reduce the climate impact compared to land application across multiple ALCA studies (Hassanein et al., 2022; Nusselder et al., 2020). Direct combustion of poultry manure to produce renewable energy was found to reduce global warming impact compared to direct land appli- cation through the avoidance of emissions of NH3, NO, and N2O from poultry manure (Billen et al., 2015), producing sustainable energy that contributes towards SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy (United Na- tions, 2015).

There remains a critical research gap to identify which set of tech- nologies minimizes key environmental impacts while maintaining eco- nomic feasibility. Moreover, there is a knowledge gap to understand the consequences of modifying the system boundary assumptions to avoid creating negative externalities in related processes by shifting the environmental burden outside the system boundary. To comprehen- sively assess the major environmental harms of direct land application of poultry manure and the potential clean energy and carbon sequestration benefits from alternative poultry manure management practices, poultry manure in incorporated into the food-energy-water-waste nexus (FEWWN). To create the FEWWN, wastes are added to the food-energy- water nexus (Garcia and You, 2016), which can be optimized to pursue maximum environmental and economic sustainability (Garcia et al., 2019; Kibler et al., 2018). Moreover, as alternative poultry manure management systems can produce valuable solid, liquid, and gaseous products, it is further necessary to consider the economic value of the converted products through a circular economy context (Geissdoerfer et al., 2017).

To address the research gaps in the context of the FEWWN and the circular economy, we introduce a multi-objective optimization frame- work that combines techno-economic assessment (TEA) and LCAs as depicted in Fig. 1. This framework is applied to a novel biomass pro- cessing problem with a focus on potential fertilizer application of solid products from poultry manure management pathways including direct land application, pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, and AD. This model is optimized according to three objective functions, maximizing the net present value, and minimizing the global warming potential (GWP) and the freshwater eutrophication potential (FE), using a weighted sums approach for facilities of different scales. This approach enables the discovery of economically sensible and environmentally sustainable management practices that align with SDGs 6, 7, and 13 and address the major environmental harms of land application of poultry manure. Evaluating this model using a novel spatial analysis for the state of New York (NYS) yields the first comparison of the life cycle type to under- stand the tradeoffs between maintaining food production, preserving water quality, and producing of renewable energy using poultry manure. It also reveals the additional impacts caused by the inclusion of markets such as upstream fertilizer markets and downstream electricity markets. A sensitivity analysis is conducted for the solutions that maximize the net present value (NPV), minimize the GWP, and a tradeoff solution to assess the economic effects of uncertainty in the implementation of optimal technologies.

Our results yield several policy implications, especially with regards to improving the economics of poultry manure management to accel- erate adoption of environmentally sustainable technologies. First, veri- fiable, high-integrity carbon offset credits should be issued by expanding carbon financing to increase the profitability of constructing pyrolysis plants to produce biochar from poultry manure and contribute towards SDG 13. For technologies like AD, innovations in environmentally sus- tainable materials for the construction of facilities should receive gov- ernment incentives to reduce the upstream and downstream environmental impacts for a technology with relatively low capital costs and low environmental impacts under an ALCA regime. As several technologies produce waste products, the upgrading of bio-oil, AP and GP into higher quality fuels or value-added products should be

Fig. 1. System boundaries and optimization framework. The life cycle optimization framework includes an attributional life cycle assessment (ALCA), a conse- quential life cycle assessment (CLCA), multi-objective optimization, and a spatial analysis case study in New York state. Material flows and relationships in the ALCA are given in dark grey arrows, and the system is bounded by the dark blue box. The CLCA is bounded by a red box, and all material flows in the ALCA are in the CLCA. The CLCA additionally contains relationships between upstream and downstream markets demarcated with purple arrows. Aligning with the multi-objective opti- mization criteria, major emissions to air are given by three light grey arrows and major emissions to water are given by three blue products by relevant system processes. Products in A3 include fertilizer, diesel, electricity, and natural gas, and are inputs to the thermochemical conversion facilities alongside poultry manure. In stage C, Facility choice as discussed in Section 1, Facility sizing as discussed in Section 2.2, alongside operating temperatures (for HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis) and loading rate (for AD) as further discussed in Section 2.1.2 are the optimization variables being considered. Products D1-D and End Uses E1-E4 are grouped for visual clarity - conversion technology-specific products and product-specific end uses are given in Section 2.1.2.

incentivized to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and increase the available revenues for poultry farms through an increased supply of renewable fuels, further integrating poultry manure management into the circular economy. Finally, innovations in the miniaturization and commercialization of pyrolysis reactors should receive government in- centives to reduce the economic barrier of the construction and adoption of farms possessing small quantities of poultry manure (~1-100 tons manure/month), which are at best on the order of US$200/ton manure. In the Materials and Methods section, the methodologies of the LCAs, TEA, multi-objective optimization, and spatial analysis are described. In the Results and Discussion section, the spatial analysis and a tradeoff analysis using a three-dimensional pareto front is conducted. This is followed by a sensitivity analysis.
6: As the demand for poultry products increases (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2013), the poultry industry is expanding and consolidating (OECD/FAO, 2022), leading to an increase in poultry wastes such as manure. Poultry manure has major environ- mental impacts when it is applied to land, primarily from greenhouse gas emissions to air and emissions of nitrogen and phosphorus to surface water (Bolan et al., 2010). While not the largest source of agricultural emissions, land application of poultry manure contributes extensively to climate change impacts by increasing soil N2O emissions (Zhou et al., 2017), and the management of poultry manure in the U.S. in 2018 emitted 5.2 million metric tons of CO2-eq (United States Department of Agriculture, 2022). To ensure that progress is made toward limiting global temperature increases as set out in the Paris Agreement (IPCC,

2023), and make progress towards the 13th Sustainable Development Goal (SDG): Climate Action (United Nations, 2015) it is imperative to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from poultry manure management. Furthermore, land application of poultry manure can lead to extensive surface water impacts through eutrophication and contamination with harmful pathogens (Moore et al., 1995). While regulations such as the Clean Water Act in the U.S. exist to protect the environment, these regulations can be ineffective at reducing emission of nutrients to sur- face waters (Environmental Protection Agency, 2023). In New York state, poultry is the second largest producer of manure phosphorus, contributing to the overapplication of phosphorus near livestock oper- ations, especially with poultry operations in Sullivan county (Swink et al., 2009). To align with the 6th United Nations SDG: Clean Water and Sanitation (United Nations, 2015), it is necessary to reduce freshwater eutrophication impacts from poultry manure.
Due to these adverse impacts, extensive research has been conducted

In AD, microorganisms transform organic material under oxygen-free conditions to yield the renewable fuel biogas and digestate containing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium - necessary nutrients for crop growth (Liu and Rajagopal, 2019; Schulte et al., 2022). Pyrol- ysis converts biomass feedstock under temperatures of 300-600 °C with low oxygen contents, yielding renewable fuels syngas and bio-oil, an aqueous phase (AP), and the carbon sequestration agent biochar (Lehmann et al., 2021). HTL and HTC both can convert biomass in water at high pressures between 10 and 25 MPa, though HTL operates at temperatures of 280-370 °C (Toor et al., 2011) and HTC operates at temperatures of 180-220 °C (Funke and Ziegler, 2010). The primary yield of HTL is an energy-dense bio-oil (Akhtar and Amin, 2011); for HTC it is the coal replacement hydrochar (Wang et al., 2018). Both processes also yield a nitrogen-rich aqueous phase (Lu al., 2018) and a gaseous phase (GP) comprised primarily of carbon dioxide (Mau et al., 2016). By interconnecting these processes, such as using the aqueous phase of pyrolysis or hydrochar from HTC as a feedstock for AD, yields from biogas can increase (Fang et al., 2020; Torri and Fabbri, 2014), maximizing the quantity of sustainable fuels that can be produced. However, the high costs of these technologies, mitigated partially through the valorization of the end products, still presents barriers to widespread adoption (Patel et al., 2016).

To evaluate the environmental sustainability of these technologies for the conversion of poultry manure, several life cycle assessments (LCA) have been conducted. Attributional LCAs (ALCA) capture impacts assigned to a certain product or service, while consequential LCAs (CLCA) additionally quantify the environmental impacts from changes to other processes through marginal economic relationships (Earles and Halog, 2011). Both techniques have drawbacks, as the system boundary in an ALCA can be seen as arbitrary, and there is a significant data collection problem for the offset processes in a CLCA (Earles and Halog, 2011). Using an ALCA, it was found that the use of pyrolysis, gasifica- tion, HTL, and HTC decreased aquatic eutrophication by 69-99% and reduced the climate change impact by 15-53% compared to direct land application (Bora et al., 2020a). In a CLCA for AD of poultry litter in a single, fixed-size facility, the eutrophication impact was found to vary

based on the biogas use cases and that a reduction in climate change impact results from avoided electricity generation and avoided peat moss production (Beausang et al., 2020). AD of poultry litter was also found to reduce the climate impact compared to land application across multiple ALCA studies (Hassanein et al., 2022; Nusselder et al., 2020). Direct combustion of poultry manure to produce renewable energy was found to reduce global warming impact compared to direct land appli- cation through the avoidance of emissions of NH3, NO, and N2O from poultry manure (Billen et al., 2015), producing sustainable energy that contributes towards SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy (United Na- tions, 2015).

There remains a critical research gap to identify which set of tech- nologies minimizes key environmental impacts while maintaining eco- nomic feasibility. Moreover, there is a knowledge gap to understand the consequences of modifying the system boundary assumptions to avoid creating negative externalities in related processes by shifting the environmental burden outside the system boundary. To comprehen- sively assess the major environmental harms of direct land application of poultry manure and the potential clean energy and carbon sequestration benefits from alternative poultry manure management practices, poultry manure in incorporated into the food-energy-water-waste nexus (FEWWN). To create the FEWWN, wastes are added to the food-energy- water nexus (Garcia and You, 2016), which can be optimized to pursue maximum environmental and economic sustainability (Garcia et al., 2019; Kibler et al., 2018). Moreover, as alternative poultry manure management systems can produce valuable solid, liquid, and gaseous products, it is further necessary to consider the economic value of the converted products through a circular economy context (Geissdoerfer et al., 2017).

To address the research gaps in the context of the FEWWN and the circular economy, we introduce a multi-objective optimization frame- work that combines techno-economic assessment (TEA) and LCAs as depicted in Fig. 1. This framework is applied to a novel biomass pro- cessing problem with a focus on potential fertilizer application of solid products from poultry manure management pathways including direct land application, pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, and AD. This model is optimized according to three objective functions, maximizing the net present value, and minimizing the global warming potential (GWP) and the freshwater eutrophication potential (FE), using a weighted sums approach for facilities of different scales. This approach enables the discovery of economically sensible and environmentally sustainable management practices that align with SDGs 6, 7, and 13 and address the major environmental harms of land application of poultry manure. Evaluating this model using a novel spatial analysis for the state of New York (NYS) yields the first comparison of the life cycle type to under- stand the tradeoffs between maintaining food production, preserving water quality, and producing of renewable energy using poultry manure. It also reveals the additional impacts caused by the inclusion of markets such as upstream fertilizer markets and downstream electricity markets. A sensitivity analysis is conducted for the solutions that maximize the net present value (NPV), minimize the GWP, and a tradeoff solution to assess the economic effects of uncertainty in the implementation of optimal technologies.

Our results yield several policy implications, especially with regards to improving the economics of poultry manure management to accel- erate adoption of environmentally sustainable technologies. First, veri- fiable, high-integrity carbon offset credits should be issued by expanding carbon financing to increase the profitability of constructing pyrolysis plants to produce biochar from poultry manure and contribute towards SDG 13. For technologies like AD, innovations in environmentally sus- tainable materials for the construction of facilities should receive gov- ernment incentives to reduce the upstream and downstream environmental impacts for a technology with relatively low capital costs and low environmental impacts under an ALCA regime. As several technologies produce waste products, the upgrading of bio-oil, AP and GP into higher quality fuels or value-added products should be

Fig. 1. System boundaries and optimization framework. The life cycle optimization framework includes an attributional life cycle assessment (ALCA), a conse- quential life cycle assessment (CLCA), multi-objective optimization, and a spatial analysis case study in New York state. Material flows and relationships in the ALCA are given in dark grey arrows, and the system is bounded by the dark blue box. The CLCA is bounded by a red box, and all material flows in the ALCA are in the CLCA. The CLCA additionally contains relationships between upstream and downstream markets demarcated with purple arrows. Aligning with the multi-objective opti- mization criteria, major emissions to air are given by three light grey arrows and major emissions to water are given by three blue products by relevant system processes. Products in A3 include fertilizer, diesel, electricity, and natural gas, and are inputs to the thermochemical conversion facilities alongside poultry manure. In stage C, Facility choice as discussed in Section 1, Facility sizing as discussed in Section 2.2, alongside operating temperatures (for HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis) and loading rate (for AD) as further discussed in Section 2.1.2 are the optimization variables being considered. Products D1-D and End Uses E1-E4 are grouped for visual clarity - conversion technology-specific products and product-specific end uses are given in Section 2.1.2.

incentivized to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and increase the available revenues for poultry farms through an increased supply of renewable fuels, further integrating poultry manure management into the circular economy. Finally, innovations in the miniaturization and commercialization of pyrolysis reactors should receive government in- centives to reduce the economic barrier of the construction and adoption of farms possessing small quantities of poultry manure (~1-100 tons manure/month), which are at best on the order of US$200/ton manure. In the Materials and Methods section, the methodologies of the LCAs, TEA, multi-objective optimization, and spatial analysis are described. In the Results and Discussion section, the spatial analysis and a tradeoff analysis using a three-dimensional pareto front is conducted. This is followed by a sensitivity analysis.
9: As the demand for poultry products increases (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2013), the poultry industry is expanding and consolidating (OECD/FAO, 2022), leading to an increase in poultry wastes such as manure. Poultry manure has major environ- mental impacts when it is applied to land, primarily from greenhouse gas emissions to air and emissions of nitrogen and phosphorus to surface water (Bolan et al., 2010). While not the largest source of agricultural emissions, land application of poultry manure contributes extensively to climate change impacts by increasing soil N2O emissions (Zhou et al., 2017), and the management of poultry manure in the U.S. in 2018 emitted 5.2 million metric tons of CO2-eq (United States Department of Agriculture, 2022). To ensure that progress is made toward limiting global temperature increases as set out in the Paris Agreement (IPCC,

2023), and make progress towards the 13th Sustainable Development Goal (SDG): Climate Action (United Nations, 2015) it is imperative to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from poultry manure management. Furthermore, land application of poultry manure can lead to extensive surface water impacts through eutrophication and contamination with harmful pathogens (Moore et al., 1995). While regulations such as the Clean Water Act in the U.S. exist to protect the environment, these regulations can be ineffective at reducing emission of nutrients to sur- face waters (Environmental Protection Agency, 2023). In New York state, poultry is the second largest producer of manure phosphorus, contributing to the overapplication of phosphorus near livestock oper- ations, especially with poultry operations in Sullivan county (Swink et al., 2009). To align with the 6th United Nations SDG: Clean Water and Sanitation (United Nations, 2015), it is necessary to reduce freshwater eutrophication impacts from poultry manure.
Due to these adverse impacts, extensive research has been conducted

on poultry manure's use as a feedstock for alternative resource recovery pathways, such as anaerobic digestion (AD) (Bolan et al., 2010; Bujoczek et al., 2000), slow pyrolysis (Baniasadi et al., 2016; Gunes et al., 2015), hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) (Ekpo 2016; Lu et al., 2018), and hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) (Hejna et al., 2022; Mau et al., 2019). In AD, microorganisms transform organic material under oxygen-free conditions to yield the renewable fuel biogas and digestate containing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium - necessary nutrients for crop growth (Liu and Rajagopal, 2019; Schulte et al., 2022). Pyrol- ysis converts biomass feedstock under temperatures of 300-600 °C with low oxygen contents, yielding renewable fuels syngas and bio-oil, an aqueous phase (AP), and the carbon sequestration agent biochar (Lehmann et al., 2021). HTL and HTC both can convert biomass in water at high pressures between 10 and 25 MPa, though HTL operates at temperatures of 280-370 °C (Toor et al., 2011) and HTC operates at temperatures of 180-220 °C (Funke and Ziegler, 2010). The primary yield of HTL is an energy-dense bio-oil (Akhtar and Amin, 2011); for HTC it is the coal replacement hydrochar (Wang et al., 2018). Both processes also yield a nitrogen-rich aqueous phase (Lu al., 2018) and a gaseous phase (GP) comprised primarily of carbon dioxide (Mau et al., 2016). By interconnecting these processes, such as using the aqueous phase of pyrolysis or hydrochar from HTC as a feedstock for AD, yields from biogas can increase (Fang et al., 2020; Torri and Fabbri, 2014), maximizing the quantity of sustainable fuels that can be produced. However, the high costs of these technologies, mitigated partially through the valorization of the end products, still presents barriers to widespread adoption (Patel et al., 2016).

To evaluate the environmental sustainability of these technologies for the conversion of poultry manure, several life cycle assessments (LCA) have been conducted. Attributional LCAs (ALCA) capture impacts assigned to a certain product or service, while consequential LCAs (CLCA) additionally quantify the environmental impacts from changes to other processes through marginal economic relationships (Earles and Halog, 2011). Both techniques have drawbacks, as the system boundary in an ALCA can be seen as arbitrary, and there is a significant data collection problem for the offset processes in a CLCA (Earles and Halog, 2011). Using an ALCA, it was found that the use of pyrolysis, gasifica- tion, HTL, and HTC decreased aquatic eutrophication by 69-99% and reduced the climate change impact by 15-53% compared to direct land application (Bora et al., 2020a). In a CLCA for AD of poultry litter in a single, fixed-size facility, the eutrophication impact was found to vary

based on the biogas use cases and that a reduction in climate change impact results from avoided electricity generation and avoided peat moss production (Beausang et al., 2020). AD of poultry litter was also found to reduce the climate impact compared to land application across multiple ALCA studies (Hassanein et al., 2022; Nusselder et al., 2020). Direct combustion of poultry manure to produce renewable energy was found to reduce global warming impact compared to direct land appli- cation through the avoidance of emissions of NH3, NO, and N2O from poultry manure (Billen et al., 2015), producing sustainable energy that contributes towards SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy (United Na- tions, 2015).

There remains a critical research gap to identify which set of tech- nologies minimizes key environmental impacts while maintaining eco- nomic feasibility. Moreover, there is a knowledge gap to understand the consequences of modifying the system boundary assumptions to avoid creating negative externalities in related processes by shifting the environmental burden outside the system boundary. To comprehen- sively assess the major environmental harms of direct land application of poultry manure and the potential clean energy and carbon sequestration benefits from alternative poultry manure management practices, poultry manure in incorporated into the food-energy-water-waste nexus (FEWWN). To create the FEWWN, wastes are added to the food-energy- water nexus (Garcia and You, 2016), which can be optimized to pursue maximum environmental and economic sustainability (Garcia et al., 2019; Kibler et al., 2018). Moreover, as alternative poultry manure management systems can produce valuable solid, liquid, and gaseous products, it is further necessary to consider the economic value of the converted products through a circular economy context (Geissdoerfer et al., 2017).

To address the research gaps in the context of the FEWWN and the circular economy, we introduce a multi-objective optimization frame- work that combines techno-economic assessment (TEA) and LCAs as depicted in Fig. 1. This framework is applied to a novel biomass pro- cessing problem with a focus on potential fertilizer application of solid products from poultry manure management pathways including direct land application, pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, and AD. This model is optimized according to three objective functions, maximizing the net present value, and minimizing the global warming potential (GWP) and the freshwater eutrophication potential (FE), using a weighted sums approach for facilities of different scales. This approach enables the discovery of economically sensible and environmentally sustainable management practices that align with SDGs 6, 7, and 13 and address the major environmental harms of land application of poultry manure. Evaluating this model using a novel spatial analysis for the state of New York (NYS) yields the first comparison of the life cycle type to under- stand the tradeoffs between maintaining food production, preserving water quality, and producing of renewable energy using poultry manure. It also reveals the additional impacts caused by the inclusion of markets such as upstream fertilizer markets and downstream electricity markets. A sensitivity analysis is conducted for the solutions that maximize the net present value (NPV), minimize the GWP, and a tradeoff solution to assess the economic effects of uncertainty in the implementation of optimal technologies.

Our results yield several policy implications, especially with regards to improving the economics of poultry manure management to accel- erate adoption of environmentally sustainable technologies. First, veri- fiable, high-integrity carbon offset credits should be issued by expanding carbon financing to increase the profitability of constructing pyrolysis plants to produce biochar from poultry manure and contribute towards SDG 13. For technologies like AD, innovations in environmentally sus- tainable materials for the construction of facilities should receive gov- ernment incentives to reduce the upstream and downstream environmental impacts for a technology with relatively low capital costs and low environmental impacts under an ALCA regime. As several technologies produce waste products, the upgrading of bio-oil, AP and GP into higher quality fuels or value-added products should be

Fig. 1. System boundaries and optimization framework. The life cycle optimization framework includes an attributional life cycle assessment (ALCA), a conse- quential life cycle assessment (CLCA), multi-objective optimization, and a spatial analysis case study in New York state. Material flows and relationships in the ALCA are given in dark grey arrows, and the system is bounded by the dark blue box. The CLCA is bounded by a red box, and all material flows in the ALCA are in the CLCA. The CLCA additionally contains relationships between upstream and downstream markets demarcated with purple arrows. Aligning with the multi-objective opti- mization criteria, major emissions to air are given by three light grey arrows and major emissions to water are given by three blue products by relevant system processes. Products in A3 include fertilizer, diesel, electricity, and natural gas, and are inputs to the thermochemical conversion facilities alongside poultry manure. In stage C, Facility choice as discussed in Section 1, Facility sizing as discussed in Section 2.2, alongside operating temperatures (for HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis) and loading rate (for AD) as further discussed in Section 2.1.2 are the optimization variables being considered. Products D1-D and End Uses E1-E4 are grouped for visual clarity - conversion technology-specific products and product-specific end uses are given in Section 2.1.2.

incentivized to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and increase the available revenues for poultry farms through an increased supply of renewable fuels, further integrating poultry manure management into the circular economy. Finally, innovations in the miniaturization and commercialization of pyrolysis reactors should receive government in- centives to reduce the economic barrier of the construction and adoption of farms possessing small quantities of poultry manure (~1-100 tons manure/month), which are at best on the order of US$200/ton manure. In the Materials and Methods section, the methodologies of the LCAs, TEA, multi-objective optimization, and spatial analysis are described. In the Results and Discussion section, the spatial analysis and a tradeoff analysis using a three-dimensional pareto front is conducted. This is followed by a sensitivity analysis.
12: As the demand for poultry products increases (Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, 2013), the poultry industry is expanding and consolidating (OECD/FAO, 2022), leading to an increase in poultry wastes such as manure. Poultry manure has major environ- mental impacts when it is applied to land, primarily from greenhouse gas emissions to air and emissions of nitrogen and phosphorus to surface water (Bolan et al., 2010). While not the largest source of agricultural emissions, land application of poultry manure contributes extensively to climate change impacts by increasing soil N2O emissions (Zhou et al., 2017), and the management of poultry manure in the U.S. in 2018 emitted 5.2 million metric tons of CO2-eq (United States Department of Agriculture, 2022). To ensure that progress is made toward limiting global temperature increases as set out in the Paris Agreement (IPCC,

2023), and make progress towards the 13th Sustainable Development Goal (SDG): Climate Action (United Nations, 2015) it is imperative to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from poultry manure management. Furthermore, land application of poultry manure can lead to extensive surface water impacts through eutrophication and contamination with harmful pathogens (Moore et al., 1995). While regulations such as the Clean Water Act in the U.S. exist to protect the environment, these regulations can be ineffective at reducing emission of nutrients to sur- face waters (Environmental Protection Agency, 2023). In New York state, poultry is the second largest producer of manure phosphorus, contributing to the overapplication of phosphorus near livestock oper- ations, especially with poultry operations in Sullivan county (Swink et al., 2009). To align with the 6th United Nations SDG: Clean Water and Sanitation (United Nations, 2015), it is necessary to reduce freshwater eutrophication impacts from poultry manure.
Due to these adverse impacts, extensive research has been conducted

To align with the 6th United Nations SDG: Clean Water and Sanitation (United Nations, 2015), it is necessary to reduce freshwater eutrophication impacts from poultry manure.
Due to these adverse impacts, extensive research has been conducted

AD|Anaerobic Digestion ALCA|Attributional Life Cycle Assessment AP|Aqueous Phase CAFO|Concentrated Animal Feeding Operation CHP|Combined Heat and Power CLCA|Consequential Life Cycle Assessment FE|Freshwater Eutrophication Potential FEWWN|Food-Energy-Water-Waste Nexus GP|Gaseous Phase GWP|Global Warming Potential HTC|Hydrothermal Carbonization HTL|Hydrothermal Liquefaction LCA|Life Cycle Assessment MILP|Mixed Integer Linear Programming MINLP|Mixed Integer Non-Linear Programming NPV|Net Present Value NYS|New York State SDG|Sustainable Development Goal TEA|Techno-Economic Analysis

on poultry manure's use as a feedstock for alternative resource recovery pathways, such as anaerobic digestion (AD) (Bolan et al., 2010; Bujoczek et al., 2000), slow pyrolysis (Baniasadi et al., 2016; Gunes et al., 2015), hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) (Ekpo 2016; Lu et al., 2018), and hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) (Hejna et al., 2022; Mau et al., 2019). In AD, microorganisms transform organic material under oxygen-free conditions to yield the renewable fuel biogas and digestate containing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium - necessary nutrients for crop growth (Liu and Rajagopal, 2019; Schulte et al., 2022). Pyrol- ysis converts biomass feedstock under temperatures of 300-600 °C with low oxygen contents, yielding renewable fuels syngas and bio-oil, an aqueous phase (AP), and the carbon sequestration agent biochar (Lehmann et al., 2021). HTL and HTC both can convert biomass in water at high pressures between 10 and 25 MPa, though HTL operates at temperatures of 280-370 °C (Toor et al., 2011) and HTC operates at temperatures of 180-220 °C (Funke and Ziegler, 2010). The primary yield of HTL is an energy-dense bio-oil (Akhtar and Amin, 2011); for HTC it is the coal replacement hydrochar (Wang et al., 2018). Both processes also yield a nitrogen-rich aqueous phase (Lu al., 2018) and a gaseous phase (GP) comprised primarily of carbon dioxide (Mau et al., 2016). By interconnecting these processes, such as using the aqueous phase of pyrolysis or hydrochar from HTC as a feedstock for AD, yields from biogas can increase (Fang et al., 2020; Torri and Fabbri, 2014), maximizing the quantity of sustainable fuels that can be produced. However, the high costs of these technologies, mitigated partially through the valorization of the end products, still presents barriers to widespread adoption (Patel et al., 2016).

To evaluate the environmental sustainability of these technologies for the conversion of poultry manure, several life cycle assessments (LCA) have been conducted. Attributional LCAs (ALCA) capture impacts assigned to a certain product or service, while consequential LCAs (CLCA) additionally quantify the environmental impacts from changes to other processes through marginal economic relationships (Earles and Halog, 2011). Both techniques have drawbacks, as the system boundary in an ALCA can be seen as arbitrary, and there is a significant data collection problem for the offset processes in a CLCA (Earles and Halog, 2011). Using an ALCA, it was found that the use of pyrolysis, gasifica- tion, HTL, and HTC decreased aquatic eutrophication by 69-99% and reduced the climate change impact by 15-53% compared to direct land application (Bora et al., 2020a). In a CLCA for AD of poultry litter in a single, fixed-size facility, the eutrophication impact was found to vary

based on the biogas use cases and that a reduction in climate change impact results from avoided electricity generation and avoided peat moss production (Beausang et al., 2020). AD of poultry litter was also found to reduce the climate impact compared to land application across multiple ALCA studies (Hassanein et al., 2022; Nusselder et al., 2020). Direct combustion of poultry manure to produce renewable energy was found to reduce global warming impact compared to direct land appli- cation through the avoidance of emissions of NH3, NO, and N2O from poultry manure (Billen et al., 2015), producing sustainable energy that contributes towards SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy (United Na- tions, 2015).

There remains a critical research gap to identify which set of tech- nologies minimizes key environmental impacts while maintaining eco- nomic feasibility. Moreover, there is a knowledge gap to understand the consequences of modifying the system boundary assumptions to avoid creating negative externalities in related processes by shifting the environmental burden outside the system boundary. To comprehen- sively assess the major environmental harms of direct land application of poultry manure and the potential clean energy and carbon sequestration benefits from alternative poultry manure management practices, poultry manure in incorporated into the food-energy-water-waste nexus (FEWWN). To create the FEWWN, wastes are added to the food-energy- water nexus (Garcia and You, 2016), which can be optimized to pursue maximum environmental and economic sustainability (Garcia et al., 2019; Kibler et al., 2018). Moreover, as alternative poultry manure management systems can produce valuable solid, liquid, and gaseous products, it is further necessary to consider the economic value of the converted products through a circular economy context (Geissdoerfer et al., 2017).

To address the research gaps in the context of the FEWWN and the circular economy, we introduce a multi-objective optimization frame- work that combines techno-economic assessment (TEA) and LCAs as depicted in Fig. 1. This framework is applied to a novel biomass pro- cessing problem with a focus on potential fertilizer application of solid products from poultry manure management pathways including direct land application, pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, and AD. This model is optimized according to three objective functions, maximizing the net present value, and minimizing the global warming potential (GWP) and the freshwater eutrophication potential (FE), using a weighted sums approach for facilities of different scales. This approach enables the discovery of economically sensible and environmentally sustainable management practices that align with SDGs 6, 7, and 13 and address the major environmental harms of land application of poultry manure. Evaluating this model using a novel spatial analysis for the state of New York (NYS) yields the first comparison of the life cycle type to under- stand the tradeoffs between maintaining food production, preserving water quality, and producing of renewable energy using poultry manure. It also reveals the additional impacts caused by the inclusion of markets such as upstream fertilizer markets and downstream electricity markets. A sensitivity analysis is conducted for the solutions that maximize the net present value (NPV), minimize the GWP, and a tradeoff solution to assess the economic effects of uncertainty in the implementation of optimal technologies.

Our results yield several policy implications, especially with regards to improving the economics of poultry manure management to accel- erate adoption of environmentally sustainable technologies. First, veri- fiable, high-integrity carbon offset credits should be issued by expanding carbon financing to increase the profitability of constructing pyrolysis plants to produce biochar from poultry manure and contribute towards SDG 13. For technologies like AD, innovations in environmentally sus- tainable materials for the construction of facilities should receive gov- ernment incentives to reduce the upstream and downstream environmental impacts for a technology with relatively low capital costs and low environmental impacts under an ALCA regime. As several technologies produce waste products, the upgrading of bio-oil, AP and GP into higher quality fuels or value-added products should be

Fig. 1. System boundaries and optimization framework. The life cycle optimization framework includes an attributional life cycle assessment (ALCA), a conse- quential life cycle assessment (CLCA), multi-objective optimization, and a spatial analysis case study in New York state. Material flows and relationships in the ALCA are given in dark grey arrows, and the system is bounded by the dark blue box. The CLCA is bounded by a red box, and all material flows in the ALCA are in the CLCA. The CLCA additionally contains relationships between upstream and downstream markets demarcated with purple arrows. Aligning with the multi-objective opti- mization criteria, major emissions to air are given by three light grey arrows and major emissions to water are given by three blue products by relevant system processes. Products in A3 include fertilizer, diesel, electricity, and natural gas, and are inputs to the thermochemical conversion facilities alongside poultry manure. In stage C, Facility choice as discussed in Section 1, Facility sizing as discussed in Section 2.2, alongside operating temperatures (for HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis) and loading rate (for AD) as further discussed in Section 2.1.2 are the optimization variables being considered. Products D1-D and End Uses E1-E4 are grouped for visual clarity - conversion technology-specific products and product-specific end uses are given in Section 2.1.2.

incentivized to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and increase the available revenues for poultry farms through an increased supply of renewable fuels, further integrating poultry manure management into the circular economy. Finally, innovations in the miniaturization and commercialization of pyrolysis reactors should receive government in- centives to reduce the economic barrier of the construction and adoption of farms possessing small quantities of poultry manure (~1-100 tons manure/month), which are at best on the order of US$200/ton manure. In the Materials and Methods section, the methodologies of the LCAs, TEA, multi-objective optimization, and spatial analysis are described. In the Results and Discussion section, the spatial analysis and a tradeoff analysis using a three-dimensional pareto front is conducted. This is followed by a sensitivity analysis.
15: 2.1. Attributional and consequential life cycle assessment of poultry manure management technologies The following subsections give a brief overview of the life cycle assessment methodologies used in this case study, including the goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, and impact assessment. 2.1.1. Goal and scope definition Two different "cradle-to-grave" LCA methods (Ekvall, 2019) are used: a cut-off ALCA to measure the environmental impacts of

processing poultry manure using these technologies; and a CLCA to measure the upstream and downstream environmental impacts of introducing these technologies and products into markets. As shown in Fig. 1, the ALCA has a smaller system boundary, as it does not consider the changes in market dynamics that are captured in the CLCA. The "cradle" for the poultry manure is its collection at a poultry farm, while the "grave" applies to the end use of products, which can be sale of products in markets, the direct land application of products, or the combustion of products, or the disposal of products. The poultry manure enters the LCA system boundary burden-free, as poultry is raised for meat and eggs, and the burden would be constant in all cases (Billen et al., 2015). For both LCAs and the TEA, the functional unit is the treatment of one metric ton of poultry manure, as it aligns with the mass balance yields reported in the literature. A 100-year LCA time horizon is chosen to harmonize with the biochar stability calculations (Leng et al., 2019) and follows other time horizons chosen for LCAs of waste to en- ergy technologies (Ahamed et al., 2016).

For both LCAs the majority of the life cycle inventory data is acquired directly from the EcoInvent database 3.9.1 (Wernet et al., 2016) using OpenLCA 1.11.0 (GreenDelta, 2023) as detailed in Section S1 in the SI. Data for processes in the ALCA is taken from the EcoInvent attributional database, whereas data for the processes in the CLCA is taken from the EcoInvent consequential database. Where possible, data specific to NYS and the U.S. was used; otherwise processes for the rest of the world were

18: used. The full life cycle inventory methods, including product allocation, is in Section S1 in the SI, and the life cycle inventory data are in Tables S15-S40 in the SI. The environmental impacts from waste and leakages are excluded due to insufficient data, though this assumption will likely lead to an underestimation of the calculated environmental impacts (Ioannou-Ttofa et al., 2021).

To acquire estimates for the spatial analysis of the supply of poultry manure in NYS, county level data from the USDA is used (United States Department of Agriculture National Agricultural Statistics Service, 2019). In locations where the total number of poultry is withheld to protect business operations, supplemental information on concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) from the NYS Geographic Informa- tion Gateway (New York Department of State Geographic Information Gateway, 2023) is used. Following methods used in the relevant litera- ture (Zhao et al., 2020) the calculated totals for poultry manure pro- duction for all 62 counties in NYS can be found in Table S5 in the SI, and it is assumed that the amount of manure produced is constant over time.

Data for the yields from the processes are taken from experiments conducted in the literature for pyrolysis (Baniasadi et al., 2016; Enders et al., 2012), HTL (Ekpo et al., 2016; Vadlamudi et al., 2024), HTC (Heilmann et al., 2014; Mau et al., 2016), and AD (Bareha et al., 2021; Beausang et al., 2020; Grimberg et al., 2015), and can be found in Tables S7, S13, S11, and S14 in the SI respectively. A brief overview of these technologies can be found in the second paragraph of the intro- duction. Gasification (Rai et al., 2022), supercritical water gasification (Yanagida et al., 2009), and other technologies are not considered, as the production of solid fertilizer products is necessary to ensure suffi- cient food production in the FEWWN. Process operating temperatures (organic loading rate for AD) are chosen as the dependent variable because there is sufficient experimental evidence for temperature-variant yields for poultry manure in the literature, though other process operating conditions exist, such as reactor type (Massalha et al., 2020), catalysts (Liu et al., 2017), and residence time (Bradley et al., 2021). As for the end uses of the products, we assume that biochar can be used as a soil amendment (Krounbi et al., 2019), fuel for CHP (Laird et al., 2009) or landfilled. Bio-oil can be combusted as a fuel oil (Janarthanam et al., 2020; Lee et al., 2014) or sold on the market (Snowden-Swan et al., 2022). Syngas and biogas can be combusted in CHP (Monlau et al., 2015; Woolf et al., 2010) or vented to the atmo- sphere. AP can be used as a feedstock for anaerobic digestion (Torri and Fabbri, 2014), or disposed of and treated as wastewater (Snowden-Swan et al., 2022). Hydrochar, like biochar, can be used as a soil amendment (Masoumi et al., 2021) or as fuel (Wang et al., 2018) for CHP, but it can also be sold in lignite markets and offset coal (Sharma et al., 2020). GP is assumed to be vented to the atmosphere (Mau et al., 2016). Digestate produced can either be landfilled or applied to land as a soil amendment (Mortola et al., 2019). Finally, poultry manure can also be applied as fertilizer directly to land in NYS without being processed by any tech- nology (Westlaw, 2021) to grow food. Further discussion of product characterization and end uses can be found in Tables S7-S14 in the SI.

20: Both types of LCA interpret the environmental assessment results based on full-spectrum ReCiPe 2016 midpoint indicators, though we select global warming potential (GWP) and freshwater eutrophication potential (FE) as optimization objectives to fight against climate change (IPCC, 2023) and align with NYS policy (NYS Dept. of Environmental Conservation, 2012; 2018) on preventing harmful algal blooms from agricultural runoff to protect water quality (Schindler et al., 2016).
21: <header>2.2. Techno-economic analysis of poultry manure management technologies </header>

Comparative TEA aims to analyze the economic performance of a set of technologies by comparing economic indicators, in this case NPV. To calculate the NPV, the model generates a series of quarterly cash flows

for 10 years of plant operation using a discount rate of 7% (KPMG, 2022), which is the average cost of capital for chemical manufacturing. All parameter values for capital costs, operational expenses, and oper- ational revenues can be found in Tables 1 and 2 in the SI, with additional TEA methods in Section S1 of the SI. Because of the difficulties involved with pricing facilities, there is assumed to be no salvage value at the end of the time horizon. All prices are converted to 2021 US$ using historical exchanges rates (OECD, 2023), the producer price index for chemical manufacturing (BLS, 2023a) for capital costs, and the consumer price index (BLS, 2023b) for operational costs and revenues. To account for uncertainty in chemical composition and variability in economic costs and revenues across GWP optimal, NPV optimal, and a tradeoff solution, a sensitivity analysis is conducted on key parameters, with best and worst cases derived from the literature as shown in Table 3 in the SI.

23: <header>2.3. Multi-objective life cycle optimization model formulation for the poultry manure food-energy-water-waste nexus </header>

As noted in Fig. 1, the three objective functions for the multi- objective optimization framework are NPV, GWP, and FE SO as to highlight the tradeoffs inherent in the FEWWN. These objective func- tions are combined using a simple weighted sums approach, as stake- holders may prefer solutions with different weighting of objective functions. For example, stakeholders may find optimal solutions with high NPV and high GWP useful because there is high economic potential in these solutions, and the stakeholders may not care about the climate impact. The models are evaluated for three facilities with different supplies of manure: the largest feasible facility identified in a facility location problem (FLP), Onondaga county, the county with the most manure in NYS with 1356 tons manure/month, and Jefferson county, a county with a small supply of manure at 76 tons manure/month. A simplified problem formulation is presented below.

S37 Life Mass Economic Input cycle balance constraints environmental constraints constraints in Eq. in S58-S83 Eq. impact in Eq. S3, S117-S232 S46-S57, assessment S84-S116 constraints in Eq. S4-S45" s.t.|"Freshwater Global Warming Eutrophication Potential in Potential Eq. S37 in Eq. S37 Life Mass Economic Input cycle balance constraints environmental constraints constraints in Eq. in S58-S83 Eq. impact in Eq. S3, S117-S232 S46-S57, assessment S84-S116 constraints in Eq. S4-S45"

The mass balance constraints track the poultry manure from input to the model to the final end product destinations and contain binary variables for choosing operating conditions. The input constraints track the consumption of additional inputs from the technosphere, like water and electricity from the grid. The economic constraints calculate the non-linear capital costs which embody economies of scale, and oper- ating costs and revenues, which culminates in the calculation of NPV. The life cycle environmental impact constraints multiply the impact factors extracted from the life cycle inventory data by material and energy flows through the system boundaries to calculate the life cycle environmental impacts. The decision variables include the size of the facility, how much manure to process in each technology in each time period, the operating conditions for each technology, how much product to store, and the eventual product end use. All sets, variables, and equations used in this model can be found in Sections S2 and S3 in the SI. For single objective optimization for one county, the model is coded in Pyomo 6.4.2 (Hart et al., 2011), resulting in a mixed-integer non-linear programming (MINLP) problem. While it is possible to solve this multi-objective MINLP problem formulation using state-of-the-art heu- ristic methods like the multi-objective slime mould algorithm (Premkumar et al., 2021), a deterministic solution returns some guar- antee of optimality. Instead, the deterministic MINLP solve Couenne 0.5.8 (Belotti, 2016) was used, but it was unable to solve the problem within 5 h on an XPS 7590 using an Intel i7-9750H CPU @ 2.60 GHz.
To decrease the computation time, the nonlinear and binary terms in

the MINLP are linearized to reformulate this problem as a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) problem. The non-linear terms are the capital cost curves, and these are linearized using piecewise linear approximation (Misener and Floudas, 2010). Binary terms with decision variables in the mass balance constraints are linearized with Glover's linearization (Glover and Woolsey, 1974). The MILP problem size is 25, 750 variables, of which 483 are integer variables. The problem can be solved using the state-of-the-art MILP solver Gurobi 9.5.2 (Gurobi Optimization LLC, 2023) in 1.68 S on an XPS 15 7590 using an Intel i7-9750H CPU @ 2.60 GHz, highlighting the computational efficacy of the linearization methods. Due to this increased computational effi- ciency, the mixed-integer linear program was used to generate results. The code for the model can be found on the github repository (htt ps://github.com/PEESEgroup/poultry-CLCO).

Because the capital cost terms are non-linear, there are opportunities to take advantage of economies of scale using a FLP (Cornuejols et al., 1983) by combining manure supply from multiple counties in a spatial analysis. To identify optimal facility placement and size at decreased computation time, an approximation to the full model that includes only capital and transportation costs for counties that produce more than 20 tons manure/month is developed. The mixed-integer linear program- ming problem formulation given in Section S4 in the SI. This model is evaluated for two, three, and four facilities, because a solution for NYS that has one facility by definition must have exceptional transportation distances, while solutions with additional facilities are unnecessary as the full model already accounts for medium-sized facilities. This model is also formulated in Python 3.8 using Pyomo 6.4.2 (Hart et al., 2011) and solved using Gurobi 9.5.2 (Gurobi Optimization LLC, 2023). The

results from the spatial analysis are presented in Section 3.1, so as to use the identified large-scale feasible facility as an example of the benefits of harnessing economies of scale in the case study.

The mass balance constraints track the poultry manure from input to the model to the final end product destinations and contain binary variables for choosing operating conditions. The input constraints track the consumption of additional inputs from the technosphere, like water and electricity from the grid. The economic constraints calculate the non-linear capital costs which embody economies of scale, and oper- ating costs and revenues, which culminates in the calculation of NPV. The life cycle environmental impact constraints multiply the impact factors extracted from the life cycle inventory data by material and energy flows through the system boundaries to calculate the life cycle environmental impacts. The decision variables include the size of the facility, how much manure to process in each technology in each time period, the operating conditions for each technology, how much product to store, and the eventual product end use. All sets, variables, and equations used in this model can be found in Sections S2 and S3 in the SI. For single objective optimization for one county, the model is coded in Pyomo 6.4.2 (Hart et al., 2011), resulting in a mixed-integer non-linear programming (MINLP) problem. While it is possible to solve this multi-objective MINLP problem formulation using state-of-the-art heu- ristic methods like the multi-objective slime mould algorithm (Premkumar et al., 2021), a deterministic solution returns some guar- antee of optimality. Instead, the deterministic MINLP solve Couenne 0.5.8 (Belotti, 2016) was used, but it was unable to solve the problem within 5 h on an XPS 7590 using an Intel i7-9750H CPU @ 2.60 GHz.
To decrease the computation time, the nonlinear and binary terms in

the MINLP are linearized to reformulate this problem as a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) problem. The non-linear terms are the capital cost curves, and these are linearized using piecewise linear approximation (Misener and Floudas, 2010). Binary terms with decision variables in the mass balance constraints are linearized with Glover's linearization (Glover and Woolsey, 1974). The MILP problem size is 25, 750 variables, of which 483 are integer variables. The problem can be solved using the state-of-the-art MILP solver Gurobi 9.5.2 (Gurobi Optimization LLC, 2023) in 1.68 S on an XPS 15 7590 using an Intel i7-9750H CPU @ 2.60 GHz, highlighting the computational efficacy of the linearization methods. Due to this increased computational effi- ciency, the mixed-integer linear program was used to generate results. The code for the model can be found on the github repository (htt ps://github.com/PEESEgroup/poultry-CLCO).

Because the capital cost terms are non-linear, there are opportunities to take advantage of economies of scale using a FLP (Cornuejols et al., 1983) by combining manure supply from multiple counties in a spatial analysis. To identify optimal facility placement and size at decreased computation time, an approximation to the full model that includes only capital and transportation costs for counties that produce more than 20 tons manure/month is developed. The mixed-integer linear program- ming problem formulation given in Section S4 in the SI. This model is evaluated for two, three, and four facilities, because a solution for NYS that has one facility by definition must have exceptional transportation distances, while solutions with additional facilities are unnecessary as the full model already accounts for medium-sized facilities. This model is also formulated in Python 3.8 using Pyomo 6.4.2 (Hart et al., 2011) and solved using Gurobi 9.5.2 (Gurobi Optimization LLC, 2023). The

results from the spatial analysis are presented in Section 3.1, so as to use the identified large-scale feasible facility as an example of the benefits of harnessing economies of scale in the case study.
25: <header>2.3. Multi-objective life cycle optimization model formulation for the poultry manure food-energy-water-waste nexus </header>

As noted in Fig. 1, the three objective functions for the multi- objective optimization framework are NPV, GWP, and FE SO as to highlight the tradeoffs inherent in the FEWWN. These objective func- tions are combined using a simple weighted sums approach, as stake- holders may prefer solutions with different weighting of objective functions. For example, stakeholders may find optimal solutions with high NPV and high GWP useful because there is high economic potential in these solutions, and the stakeholders may not care about the climate impact. The models are evaluated for three facilities with different supplies of manure: the largest feasible facility identified in a facility location problem (FLP), Onondaga county, the county with the most manure in NYS with 1356 tons manure/month, and Jefferson county, a county with a small supply of manure at 76 tons manure/month. A simplified problem formulation is presented below.

28: 3.1. Spatial analysis in New York State

Poultry manure supply for the case study of NYS is geographically dispersed, as shown in Fig. 2a, with a greater concentration of poultry manure in the western half of the state. There are several counties with no poultry manure production, while Onondaga county produces 1356 tons manure/month, the most of any county. Counties with high levels of manure typically have several CAFOs, while counties that have minimal manure have no CAFOs but hundreds of small poultry operations.

To identify the largest feasible facilities that could be constructed, three different optimal spatial distributions of large facilities are shown above. In Fig. 2b there is the optimal solution for two facilities, a large facility in the west just south of Lake Ontario that can process 5767 ton manure/month with an average transportation distance of 111.5 km. However, counties in the southeast are not well served by this solution, with exceptional average transportation distances of several hundreds of kilometers. In the 3-facility solution in Fig. 2c, the eastern facility is split into two, with one facility serving southeast NYS and processing 1061 ton manure/month and one serving Franklin and Clinton counties and processing 1035 ton manure/month with an average transportation distance of 19.2 km. Finally, in the 4-facility solution in Fig. 2d, Erie and

Fig. 2. Spatial analysis of poultry manure in New York state. a) Manure production in tons per month for each county in the state. The optimal locations when: b) two facilities are built; c) three facilities are built; d) four facilities are built. The red arrows in sub-figures b-d show the transportation of manure to processing facilities in non-contiguous counties.

Genesee counties in western NYS gain their own facility to process 1059 ton manure/month at an average transportation distance of 15.2 km. As more facilities are being built, the trend is to construct them in areas with CAFOs in several nearby counties, drastically reducing trans- portation distances from cases with fewer facilities.

The long transportation distances call into question the operational feasibility of using the largest facility in the two-facility solution in NYS from Fig. 2b. Using Euclidean distances, average transportation dis- tances for most large facilities are close to 100 km, and due to geographic obstacles, such as the Finger Lakes and Adirondack moun- tains, transportation distances are likely underestimated. Its use in the following sections represents an optimistic best case for harnessing economies of scale in NYS. However, for certain pairs of counties, for

example Erie & Genesee and Franklin & Clinton, it may prove beneficial to construct one large facility in place of two smaller ones due to min- imal transportation distances to take advantage of economies of scale. In locations with intensive poultry farming, the profitability of the tech- nologies is almost certain to improve due to economic scaling effects, SO long as transportation distances are kept short.

In locations with intensive poultry farming, the profitability of the tech- nologies is almost certain to improve due to economic scaling effects, SO long as transportation distances are kept short.
33: <header>3.2. Optimal technologies change depending on scale and LCA system boundary </header>

The optimal three-dimensional pareto front contour results for the largest feasible facility in NYS is given in Fig. 3, with the optimal pareto points given in Fig. S3 in the SI. For the largest feasible facility in NYS,
