processing 5767 ton manure/month, the CLCA front is dominated by two main technologies, pyrolysis, and direct land application. Pyrolysis contains the NPV maximizing points at US$19.45/ton manure, which have FE values of greater than 2.5 kg P-eq/ton manure and GWP values of greater than 500 kg CO2-eq/ton manure, due to the high GWP impact from combusting biochar and high FE impact from the bio-oil offset. From this point, to reduce GWP more biochar is applied to land, and to reduce FE HTC with the sale of hydrochar as a coal offset is imple- mented, with a minimum FE of -0.96 kg P-eq/ton manure. Direct land application results in a GWP yield of -20 kg CO2-eq/ton manure due to the consideration of fertilizer offsets, with a FE impact of 0.502 kg P-eq/ ton manure, primarily from land application of manure. However, direct land application is by far the most profitable technology to achieve minimal FE impact at scale, with an NPV of US$7.75/ton manure.

Moving to Onondaga and Jefferson counties, pyrolysis and HTC be- comes significantly less profitable due to the relative increase in capital costs on a per ton basis derived from economies of scale, allowing direct land application to maximize NPV at US$21.03/ton manure and US $19.70/ton manure. The slight discrepancy in NPV values is caused by varied transportation distances in each county. For technologies that are not direct land application, the NPV is lower in Jefferson county due to diseconomies of scale, while the GWP and FE remain similar. To increase the profitability of HTC and encourage adoption of an FE minimization technology, further upgrades for AP and GP should be considered, as these products do not contribute to NPV as a source of revenue in the optimal solution. For both Onondaga and Jefferson counties, the other solution mirrors those found in the CLCA for the largest facility, with HTC with sale of hydrochar minimizing FE and pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar minimizing GWP, as shown in Fig. 3c and e. Optimal objective function values for Onondaga county can be found in Table 1.

Pyrolysis is the technology that minimizes GWP in the CLCA, pri- marily due to the well-documented carbon sequestration effects of bio- char. Pyrolysis can offer improvements in GWP of approximately 1.5 tons CO2-eq/ton manure in all three counties compared to the base case of direct land application, with variation between counties primarily deriving from differing transportation distances. While HTC minimizes FE, this is primarily accomplished by an offset of coal. Notwithstanding the high economic costs of this solution, poultry manure management can be made more sustainable through reducing emissions to water by 1.463 kg P-eq/ton manure by switching from direct land application to HTC in Onondaga county. Therefore, in the CLCA context, pyrolysis is the pathway best positioned to aid the achievement of SDG 13: Climate Action, and HTC is best positioned to aid the achievement of SDG 6:

Clean Water and Sanitation. Moreover, the renewable sources of energy produced using pyrolysis and HTC aid in attaining SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy.

The ALCA system boundary ignores the large impacts posed by off- sets for hydrochar and bio-oil because they lie outside the system boundary. Therefore, for the largest facility in NYS, FE minimization is accomplished using AD instead of HTC. There are two optimal AD so- lutions, one which generates revenue from biogas, and one which does not. AD is considered an optimal solution in the ALCA because of the significantly lower environmental emissions from digester construction, implying that facility managers should choose digesters that come from sustainable supply chains. A revenue generating AD facility produces 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure, but by deliberately avoiding revenue gen- eration through venting biogas to the atmosphere and reducing the product applied to land, FE impact can be reduced to 0.002 kg P-eq/ton manure at the cost of NPV of US$81/ton manure and an additional emittance of 4989 kg CO2-eq/ton manure. The NPV maximization so- lution uses pyrolysis, combusts biochar, and sells bio-oil, with the AP from pyrolysis used as a feedstock for AD to generate additional revenue. Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, as in the CLCA, minimizes GWP. For this facility size, there are readily apparent tradeoff solutions that include pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar, offering minimal GWP and FE impacts at a reasonable NPV of -US$7/ton manure to US$13/ton manure, highlighting the environmental benefits of py- rolysis at an affordable price.

For Onondaga county in the ALCA, the NPV maximization solution is direct land application, which corresponds to the highest FE impact. As in Table 2, AD with CHP of biogas has an NPV of -US$2.12/ton manure, with a FE impact of 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure and a GWP of 80 kg CO2- eq/ton manure. As in the largest facility, pyrolysis continues to domi- nate the GWP minimization solutions, and AD the FE minimization so- lution, though the NPV are decreased due to the scaling effects of capital, as shown in Fig. 3d and f. For Jefferson county, the technologies are identical to those in Onondaga county, with small perturbations in environmental effects due to capital costs and transportation. Therefore, achieving economies of scale is essential to achieving profitability for these alternative technologies.

Pyrolysis minimizes GWP and nearly minimizes FE in the ALCA, with a reduction GWP of approximately 0.9 tons CO2-eq/ton manure and 0.51 kg P-eq/ton manure in Onondaga county compared to the base case of direct land application. While AD minimizes FE, this is primarily
Table 2
Objective function values for optimal solutions for Onondaga county in ALCA context.

Solution Description|NPV (US $/ton manure)|GWP (kg CO2- eq/ton manure)|FE (kg P-eq/ ton manure) Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, sale of bio-oil, CHP of syngas, disposal of AP. And HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP.|-118|-1|-0.528 HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP|-149|277|-0.965 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 500 °C.|-19|-1604|3.468 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 450 °C.|-22|-1535|3.121 Direct land application of poultry manure.|21|-46|0.498

accomplished by disposing of the products of AD and not using them as a source of revenue, significantly increasing the costs of this solution. However, if revenues are earned from the products of AD, using AD can result in a reduction in FE of 0.515 kg P-eq/ton manure and a reduction in GWP of 26 kg CO2-eq/ton manure compared to direct land application in Onondaga county. This highlights the environmental sustainability of using AD, which is made potentially economically feasible in Onondaga county with an NPV of -US$2/ton manure. In the ALCA, AD is the pathway with the most promise of achieving SDG 6, while pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar shows the most promise of achieving SDG 13. The renewable sources of energy produced using pyrolysis and AD aid in attaining SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy.

For GWP reduction and profitability at scales, pyrolysis should be adopted because of the high revenue generated by bio-oil and the carbon sequestration potential of biochar. However, more revenue is generated when biochar is combusted, necessitating carbon credits of at least US $33/ton CO2-eq in an ALCA setting to ensure land application of biochar or regulations restricting its combustion. For NPV maximization at small scales, direct land application should be chosen due to low capital and operating costs, though the optimal selection is dependent on the chosen system boundary and sensitive to modeling assumptions and economies of scale. Economies of scale have a large impact on the optimal solutions, with the shape of the Pareto front changing in both the ALCA and CLCA case between the largest feasible facility and Onondaga county.

However, direct land application is by far the most profitable technology to achieve minimal FE impact at scale, with an NPV of US$7.75/ton manure.

Moving to Onondaga and Jefferson counties, pyrolysis and HTC be- comes significantly less profitable due to the relative increase in capital costs on a per ton basis derived from economies of scale, allowing direct land application to maximize NPV at US$21.03/ton manure and US $19.70/ton manure. The slight discrepancy in NPV values is caused by varied transportation distances in each county. For technologies that are not direct land application, the NPV is lower in Jefferson county due to diseconomies of scale, while the GWP and FE remain similar. To increase the profitability of HTC and encourage adoption of an FE minimization technology, further upgrades for AP and GP should be considered, as these products do not contribute to NPV as a source of revenue in the optimal solution. For both Onondaga and Jefferson counties, the other solution mirrors those found in the CLCA for the largest facility, with HTC with sale of hydrochar minimizing FE and pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar minimizing GWP, as shown in Fig. 3c and e. Optimal objective function values for Onondaga county can be found in Table 1.

Pyrolysis is the technology that minimizes GWP in the CLCA, pri- marily due to the well-documented carbon sequestration effects of bio- char. Pyrolysis can offer improvements in GWP of approximately 1.5 tons CO2-eq/ton manure in all three counties compared to the base case of direct land application, with variation between counties primarily deriving from differing transportation distances. While HTC minimizes FE, this is primarily accomplished by an offset of coal. Notwithstanding the high economic costs of this solution, poultry manure management can be made more sustainable through reducing emissions to water by 1.463 kg P-eq/ton manure by switching from direct land application to HTC in Onondaga county. Therefore, in the CLCA context, pyrolysis is the pathway best positioned to aid the achievement of SDG 13: Climate Action, and HTC is best positioned to aid the achievement of SDG 6:

The ALCA system boundary ignores the large impacts posed by off- sets for hydrochar and bio-oil because they lie outside the system boundary. Therefore, for the largest facility in NYS, FE minimization is accomplished using AD instead of HTC. There are two optimal AD so- lutions, one which generates revenue from biogas, and one which does not. AD is considered an optimal solution in the ALCA because of the significantly lower environmental emissions from digester construction, implying that facility managers should choose digesters that come from sustainable supply chains. A revenue generating AD facility produces 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure, but by deliberately avoiding revenue gen- eration through venting biogas to the atmosphere and reducing the product applied to land, FE impact can be reduced to 0.002 kg P-eq/ton manure at the cost of NPV of US$81/ton manure and an additional emittance of 4989 kg CO2-eq/ton manure. The NPV maximization so- lution uses pyrolysis, combusts biochar, and sells bio-oil, with the AP from pyrolysis used as a feedstock for AD to generate additional revenue. Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, as in the CLCA, minimizes GWP. For this facility size, there are readily apparent tradeoff solutions that include pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar, offering minimal GWP and FE impacts at a reasonable NPV of -US$7/ton manure to US$13/ton manure, highlighting the environmental benefits of py- rolysis at an affordable price.

For Onondaga county in the ALCA, the NPV maximization solution is direct land application, which corresponds to the highest FE impact. As in Table 2, AD with CHP of biogas has an NPV of -US$2.12/ton manure, with a FE impact of 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure and a GWP of 80 kg CO2- eq/ton manure. As in the largest facility, pyrolysis continues to domi- nate the GWP minimization solutions, and AD the FE minimization so- lution, though the NPV are decreased due to the scaling effects of capital, as shown in Fig. 3d and f. For Jefferson county, the technologies are identical to those in Onondaga county, with small perturbations in environmental effects due to capital costs and transportation. Therefore, achieving economies of scale is essential to achieving profitability for these alternative technologies.

Pyrolysis minimizes GWP and nearly minimizes FE in the ALCA, with a reduction GWP of approximately 0.9 tons CO2-eq/ton manure and 0.51 kg P-eq/ton manure in Onondaga county compared to the base case of direct land application. While AD minimizes FE, this is primarily

Objective function values for optimal solutions for Onondaga county in ALCA context.

Solution Description|NPV (US $/ton manure)|GWP (kg CO2- eq/ton manure)|FE (kg P-eq/ ton manure) Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, sale of bio-oil, CHP of syngas, disposal of AP. And HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP.|-118|-1|-0.528 HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP|-149|277|-0.965 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 500 °C.|-19|-1604|3.468 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 450 °C.|-22|-1535|3.121 Direct land application of poultry manure.|21|-46|0.498

accomplished by disposing of the products of AD and not using them as a source of revenue, significantly increasing the costs of this solution. However, if revenues are earned from the products of AD, using AD can result in a reduction in FE of 0.515 kg P-eq/ton manure and a reduction in GWP of 26 kg CO2-eq/ton manure compared to direct land application in Onondaga county. This highlights the environmental sustainability of using AD, which is made potentially economically feasible in Onondaga county with an NPV of -US$2/ton manure. In the ALCA, AD is the pathway with the most promise of achieving SDG 6, while pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar shows the most promise of achieving SDG 13. The renewable sources of energy produced using pyrolysis and AD aid in attaining SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy.

For GWP reduction and profitability at scales, pyrolysis should be adopted because of the high revenue generated by bio-oil and the carbon sequestration potential of biochar. However, more revenue is generated when biochar is combusted, necessitating carbon credits of at least US $33/ton CO2-eq in an ALCA setting to ensure land application of biochar or regulations restricting its combustion. For NPV maximization at small scales, direct land application should be chosen due to low capital and operating costs, though the optimal selection is dependent on the chosen system boundary and sensitive to modeling assumptions and economies of scale. Economies of scale have a large impact on the optimal solutions, with the shape of the Pareto front changing in both the ALCA and CLCA case between the largest feasible facility and Onondaga county.

The ALCA system boundary ignores the large impacts posed by off- sets for hydrochar and bio-oil because they lie outside the system boundary. Therefore, for the largest facility in NYS, FE minimization is accomplished using AD instead of HTC. There are two optimal AD so- lutions, one which generates revenue from biogas, and one which does not. AD is considered an optimal solution in the ALCA because of the significantly lower environmental emissions from digester construction, implying that facility managers should choose digesters that come from sustainable supply chains. A revenue generating AD facility produces 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure, but by deliberately avoiding revenue gen- eration through venting biogas to the atmosphere and reducing the product applied to land, FE impact can be reduced to 0.002 kg P-eq/ton manure at the cost of NPV of US$81/ton manure and an additional emittance of 4989 kg CO2-eq/ton manure. The NPV maximization so- lution uses pyrolysis, combusts biochar, and sells bio-oil, with the AP from pyrolysis used as a feedstock for AD to generate additional revenue. Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, as in the CLCA, minimizes GWP. For this facility size, there are readily apparent tradeoff solutions that include pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar, offering minimal GWP and FE impacts at a reasonable NPV of -US$7/ton manure to US$13/ton manure, highlighting the environmental benefits of py- rolysis at an affordable price.

For Onondaga county in the ALCA, the NPV maximization solution is direct land application, which corresponds to the highest FE impact. As in Table 2, AD with CHP of biogas has an NPV of -US$2.12/ton manure, with a FE impact of 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure and a GWP of 80 kg CO2- eq/ton manure. As in the largest facility, pyrolysis continues to domi- nate the GWP minimization solutions, and AD the FE minimization so- lution, though the NPV are decreased due to the scaling effects of capital, as shown in Fig. 3d and f. For Jefferson county, the technologies are identical to those in Onondaga county, with small perturbations in environmental effects due to capital costs and transportation. Therefore, achieving economies of scale is essential to achieving profitability for these alternative technologies.

Pyrolysis minimizes GWP and nearly minimizes FE in the ALCA, with a reduction GWP of approximately 0.9 tons CO2-eq/ton manure and 0.51 kg P-eq/ton manure in Onondaga county compared to the base case of direct land application. While AD minimizes FE, this is primarily

Solution Description|NPV (US $/ton manure)|GWP (kg CO2- eq/ton manure)|FE (kg P-eq/ ton manure) Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, sale of bio-oil, CHP of syngas, disposal of AP. And HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP.|-118|-1|-0.528 HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP|-149|277|-0.965 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 500 °C.|-19|-1604|3.468 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 450 °C.|-22|-1535|3.121 Direct land application of poultry manure.|21|-46|0.498

accomplished by disposing of the products of AD and not using them as a source of revenue, significantly increasing the costs of this solution. However, if revenues are earned from the products of AD, using AD can result in a reduction in FE of 0.515 kg P-eq/ton manure and a reduction in GWP of 26 kg CO2-eq/ton manure compared to direct land application in Onondaga county. This highlights the environmental sustainability of using AD, which is made potentially economically feasible in Onondaga county with an NPV of -US$2/ton manure. In the ALCA, AD is the pathway with the most promise of achieving SDG 6, while pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar shows the most promise of achieving SDG 13. The renewable sources of energy produced using pyrolysis and AD aid in attaining SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy.

For GWP reduction and profitability at scales, pyrolysis should be adopted because of the high revenue generated by bio-oil and the carbon sequestration potential of biochar. However, more revenue is generated when biochar is combusted, necessitating carbon credits of at least US $33/ton CO2-eq in an ALCA setting to ensure land application of biochar or regulations restricting its combustion. For NPV maximization at small scales, direct land application should be chosen due to low capital and operating costs, though the optimal selection is dependent on the chosen system boundary and sensitive to modeling assumptions and economies of scale. Economies of scale have a large impact on the optimal solutions, with the shape of the Pareto front changing in both the ALCA and CLCA case between the largest feasible facility and Onondaga county.

processing 5767 ton manure/month, the CLCA front is dominated by two main technologies, pyrolysis, and direct land application. Pyrolysis contains the NPV maximizing points at US$19.45/ton manure, which have FE values of greater than 2.5 kg P-eq/ton manure and GWP values of greater than 500 kg CO2-eq/ton manure, due to the high GWP impact from combusting biochar and high FE impact from the bio-oil offset. From this point, to reduce GWP more biochar is applied to land, and to reduce FE HTC with the sale of hydrochar as a coal offset is imple- mented, with a minimum FE of -0.96 kg P-eq/ton manure. Direct land application results in a GWP yield of -20 kg CO2-eq/ton manure due to the consideration of fertilizer offsets, with a FE impact of 0.502 kg P-eq/ ton manure, primarily from land application of manure. However, direct land application is by far the most profitable technology to achieve minimal FE impact at scale, with an NPV of US$7.75/ton manure.

Moving to Onondaga and Jefferson counties, pyrolysis and HTC be- comes significantly less profitable due to the relative increase in capital costs on a per ton basis derived from economies of scale, allowing direct land application to maximize NPV at US$21.03/ton manure and US $19.70/ton manure. The slight discrepancy in NPV values is caused by varied transportation distances in each county. For technologies that are not direct land application, the NPV is lower in Jefferson county due to diseconomies of scale, while the GWP and FE remain similar. To increase the profitability of HTC and encourage adoption of an FE minimization technology, further upgrades for AP and GP should be considered, as these products do not contribute to NPV as a source of revenue in the optimal solution. For both Onondaga and Jefferson counties, the other solution mirrors those found in the CLCA for the largest facility, with HTC with sale of hydrochar minimizing FE and pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar minimizing GWP, as shown in Fig. 3c and e. Optimal objective function values for Onondaga county can be found in Table 1.

Pyrolysis is the technology that minimizes GWP in the CLCA, pri- marily due to the well-documented carbon sequestration effects of bio- char. Pyrolysis can offer improvements in GWP of approximately 1.5 tons CO2-eq/ton manure in all three counties compared to the base case of direct land application, with variation between counties primarily deriving from differing transportation distances. While HTC minimizes FE, this is primarily accomplished by an offset of coal. Notwithstanding the high economic costs of this solution, poultry manure management can be made more sustainable through reducing emissions to water by 1.463 kg P-eq/ton manure by switching from direct land application to HTC in Onondaga county. Therefore, in the CLCA context, pyrolysis is the pathway best positioned to aid the achievement of SDG 13: Climate Action, and HTC is best positioned to aid the achievement of SDG 6:

A revenue generating AD facility produces 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure, but by deliberately avoiding revenue gen- eration through venting biogas to the atmosphere and reducing the product applied to land, FE impact can be reduced to 0.002 kg P-eq/ton manure at the cost of NPV of US$81/ton manure and an additional emittance of 4989 kg CO2-eq/ton manure. The NPV maximization so- lution uses pyrolysis, combusts biochar, and sells bio-oil, with the AP from pyrolysis used as a feedstock for AD to generate additional revenue. Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, as in the CLCA, minimizes GWP. For this facility size, there are readily apparent tradeoff solutions that include pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar, offering minimal GWP and FE impacts at a reasonable NPV of -US$7/ton manure to US$13/ton manure, highlighting the environmental benefits of py- rolysis at an affordable price.

For Onondaga county in the ALCA, the NPV maximization solution is direct land application, which corresponds to the highest FE impact. As in Table 2, AD with CHP of biogas has an NPV of -US$2.12/ton manure, with a FE impact of 0.010 kg P-eq/ton manure and a GWP of 80 kg CO2- eq/ton manure. As in the largest facility, pyrolysis continues to domi- nate the GWP minimization solutions, and AD the FE minimization so- lution, though the NPV are decreased due to the scaling effects of capital, as shown in Fig. 3d and f. For Jefferson county, the technologies are identical to those in Onondaga county, with small perturbations in environmental effects due to capital costs and transportation. Therefore, achieving economies of scale is essential to achieving profitability for these alternative technologies.

Pyrolysis minimizes GWP and nearly minimizes FE in the ALCA, with a reduction GWP of approximately 0.9 tons CO2-eq/ton manure and 0.51 kg P-eq/ton manure in Onondaga county compared to the base case of direct land application. While AD minimizes FE, this is primarily

Objective function values for optimal solutions for Onondaga county in ALCA context.

Solution Description|NPV (US $/ton manure)|GWP (kg CO2- eq/ton manure)|FE (kg P-eq/ ton manure) Pyrolysis with land application of biochar, sale of bio-oil, CHP of syngas, disposal of AP. And HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP.|-118|-1|-0.528 HTC with sale of hydrochar, disposal of AP and GP|-149|277|-0.965 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 500 °C.|-19|-1604|3.468 Pyrolysis with land application of biochar and selling bio-oil, CHP of syngas and disposal of AP at 450 °C.|-22|-1535|3.121 Direct land application of poultry manure.|21|-46|0.498

accomplished by disposing of the products of AD and not using them as a source of revenue, significantly increasing the costs of this solution. However, if revenues are earned from the products of AD, using AD can result in a reduction in FE of 0.515 kg P-eq/ton manure and a reduction in GWP of 26 kg CO2-eq/ton manure compared to direct land application in Onondaga county. This highlights the environmental sustainability of using AD, which is made potentially economically feasible in Onondaga county with an NPV of -US$2/ton manure. In the ALCA, AD is the pathway with the most promise of achieving SDG 6, while pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar shows the most promise of achieving SDG 13. The renewable sources of energy produced using pyrolysis and AD aid in attaining SDG 7: Affordable and Clean Energy.

For GWP reduction and profitability at scales, pyrolysis should be adopted because of the high revenue generated by bio-oil and the carbon sequestration potential of biochar. However, more revenue is generated when biochar is combusted, necessitating carbon credits of at least US $33/ton CO2-eq in an ALCA setting to ensure land application of biochar or regulations restricting its combustion. For NPV maximization at small scales, direct land application should be chosen due to low capital and operating costs, though the optimal selection is dependent on the chosen system boundary and sensitive to modeling assumptions and economies of scale. Economies of scale have a large impact on the optimal solutions, with the shape of the Pareto front changing in both the ALCA and CLCA case between the largest feasible facility and Onondaga county.
43: To compare the TEA findings in this model with those reported in the literature, we calculate the necessary biochar breakeven price. This ranges from US$61.66/ton biochar to US$498/ton biochar for large pyrolysis facilities in NYS, with a distribution shown in Fig. 4. The lower bound on these prices is almost identical to the inflation-adjusted US $62/ton biochar, but the upper bound is much higher than the upper bound of US$153/ton biochar that was calculated for a slow pyrolysis process in an ALCA for the treatment of poultry manure in NYS (Bora et al., 2020b). Yates county, which processes 907 ton manure/month or 1.37 ton manure/hour, would result in a biochar breakeven price of US $108.64/ton biochar, significantly smaller than the breakeven price of £176/ton biochar (US$330/ton biochar) found in a TEA of a pyrolysis plant operating at 1.5 ton manure/hour in Northern Ireland (Huang

Fig. 4. Biochar breakeven price comparison between this work, the slow py- rolysis from Bora et al. (Bora et al., 2020b), and Huang et al. (Huang et al., 2014). The mean value of each work is indicated by a black X.
et al., 2014), which is primarily due to the consideration of revenues from bio-oil.

As for the LCAs, environmental impacts of technologies are highly dependent upon modeling assumptions. Our results agree with the finding that GWP and FE of AD is lower than that of direct land appli- cation under certain scenarios with extra substrate input in a cradle-to- grave ALCA of AD of poultry litter (Hassanein et al., 2022). The lower GWP and FE impacts of AD compared to direct land application also agree with the findings in an ALCA of co-digestion of poultry litter with fodder maize in the Netherlands, which are that co-digestion leads to lower FE and GWP than direct land application (Nusselder et al., 2020). When applying biochar to land, pyrolysis has lower GWP impacts than land application, in agreement with a cradle-to-grave ALCA of poultry manure management technologies in NYS (Bora et al., 2020a). FE of pyrolysis in the ALCA is also lower than that of land application, agreeing with results in the literature (Bora et al., 2020a). CLCA results are not compared due to a lack of CLCA of poultry manure studies in the literature.

46: To account for uncertainties in chemical and economic parameters, a sensitivity analysis is conducted on NPV for three optimal solutions along the NPV and GWP curve as climate impacts vary significantly depending upon the fate of biochar. The parameter values used in the sensitivity analysis can be found in Table S3 of the supplemental in- formation, and primarily contain economic variables as uncertainty values for LCA impacts were generally not present in the LCA data. At the solution that maximizes NPV, all manure is applied directly to land, as shown in Fig. 3c-f and Tables 1 and 2. Because of the extremely high capital costs for Jefferson county on a per ton basis resulting from dis- economies of scale, and because some manure is still applied to land in the tradeoff solutions in Fig. S7, counties that produce less than 100 ton manure/month are excluded from pyrolysis production at the tradeoff point and instead apply poultry manure to land. At the GWP minimi- zation point, all counties that produce more than 1.07 ton manure/ month use pyrolysis, otherwise manure is applied to land. The results of the analysis can be found in Fig. 5.

The NPV maximization solution is highly affected by variables that are related to fertilizer application. Meanwhile the tradeoff solution and GWP minimization solution exhibit similar behavior and are more influenced by changes to costs than changes to revenues. The factors that greatly affect the NPV optimization solution are the price of fertilizer and the time horizon of the cash flows. Because revenues for direct land application depend primarily on fertilizer offsets, the NPV maximization solution is much more sensitive to a significant decrease in fertilizer prices than other solutions. This sensitivity to fertilizer in the NPV maximization solution is mirrored in the sensitivity analysis results for the N, P, and K content of manure and other solid products. Cash flows are positive for the NPV maximization solution as there is greater rev- enue from avoiding fertilizer than operational expenses, shortening the time horizon decreases the NPV, and lengthening the time horizon in- creases the NPV. In the tradeoff and GWP minimization solutions, the operating cash flows are near zero, resulting in less sensitivity to the time horizon of the study. This trend also holds true for changes to the interest rate, as increasing the discounting of a cash flow near zero re- sults in a smaller change to NPV than the increased discounting of a large cash flow. Due to the significant capital and operating costs, the tradeoff and GWP minimization solutions exhibit markedly higher sensitivity than direct land application to changes in CAPEX and OPEX cost variables. While there is some upside to selling electricity at higher rates or increasing the energy content of bio-oil, that alone is insufficient to guarantee profitability for pyrolysis in the tradeoff and GWP mini- mization solution. Instead, reductions in capital and operational costs are also necessary to ensure widespread profitability and adoption of pyrolysis of poultry manure in NYS.

Fig. 5. Tornado plot for the results from the sensitivity analysis of three optimal solutions for county-level facility construction in NYS. Worst denotes the worst-case scenario, and Best denotes the best-case scenario for changes to parameter values. CAPEX denotes a constant multiplier to capital costs, and OPEX denotes a constant multiplier to fixed operational costs. The base case NPV for NPV max is US$18,172,432, for tradeoff it is -US$36,570,925, and for GWP min it is -US$64,458,720. The nominal values and units for each input parameter can be found in Table S3 in the SI.

49: By applying a novel combined techno-economic and consequential life cycle optimization model to poultry manure management technol- logies including direct land application, pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, and AD, we found that either direct land application, HTC, AD, or pyrolysis could be optimal technologies depending on the economic and/or environmental goals. When discounting the economic objectives, HTC and AD offer significant process improvements for handling poultry manure over direct land application through reduced emissions of phosphorus and nitrogen to water, helping achieve SDG 6. Meanwhile, pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar significantly reduces the carbon emissions and increases the supply of clean energy from poultry manure treatment compared to direct land application, contributing to SDG 7 and 13. The economic and environmental data assumptions, including offsets in the CLCA, may limit the life cycle optimization approach by introducing additional sources of uncertainty, though this is partially ameliorated through the sensitivity analysis. The NPV maximization solution was primarily impacted by variables affecting the relationship between poultry manure and its fertilizer offset, while the tradeoff and GWP minimization solutions have higher sensitivity to changes to CAPEX and OPEX costs. Moreover, these findings are limited by the life cycle optimization methodology; for example, the selection of the sys- tem boundary to include wastes and leakages or the processes which produce poultry manure could significantly alter the environmental results of the study.

The environmental impact of technologies is significantly affected by the end uses of the products. The difference between combusting and applying biochar is the most prominent example, with significantly higher greenhouse gas emissions in the former. To incentivize land application of biochar, carbon financing could be implemented in NYS to ensure the biochar is not combusted at a minimum cost of US$33/ton CO2-eq. Because high capital costs and reactor material sourcing are the biggest obstacles to sustainable commercialization of technologies, further research into reactor miniaturization and green supply chains should be conducted. This holds especially true for AD, as facility con- struction contributes an additional 1.27 tons CO2-eq/ton manure pro- cessed in the CLCA compared to the ALCA. To offset the capital costs through increased revenue generation, high value uses for products such as AP and GP can further close the circular economy for poultry manure.

56: In the appendix, a detailed overview of the contents of the lengthy supplemental information is given. In Section S1 of the Supplemental Information, further information on the methods used for the TEA, spatial analysis, mass balance, and LCA is included. For the TEA, the sources and parameter values for the CAPEX and OPEX costs are discussed, alongside sources and parameter values for the operational revenues of facility operation. The CAPEX cost curves are built on economies of scale models, and different parameters are derived for each type of technology. OPEX costs are broken down

into fixed costs, which include primarily plant management costs and are represented as a proportion of CAPEX costs, and variable costs, which are associated with the costs of inputs. This is followed by an overview of the economics parameters used in the sensitivity analysis, including the low, high, and nominal values of the parameters and their sources. Values in the sensitivity analysis are primarily economic in nature, although the nutrient contents of solid products are also included to model different offsets for fertilizers. Moreover, this section contains information on the spatial analysis, including the calculations to derive the supply of poultry manure on a county-level basis and the calculations to derive the average transportation distances for each county. This section also contains information on the different potential operating conditions of the different technologies of poultry manure treatment facility, which are technology dependent. For HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis, this is operating temperature, while for AD, this is organic loading rate. Product yields and product characteristics for pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, AD, and CHP under different operating conditions are also presented. Finally, this section includes the methods used to conduct the LCAs. A brief list of the different EcoInvent processes used to derive impact factors for the LCAs is given, followed by a description of the end- of-life stage of different products. The relevant impact factors for the ALCA and CLCA for all processes are then given in 25 tables.

In Section S2, the notation for the life cycle optimization model is given, including sets and indices, binary variables, non-negative continuous variables, continuous variables, and parameters. For the variables, the binary variables are the process operating conditions, the continuous variables are the NPV and LCA impacts, and the non- negative continuous variables are all other variables in the model. The nomenclature for the parameter values is presented here, the actual parameter values are scattered throughout the tables in Section S1 of the supplemental information.

In Section S3, the equations for the life cycle optimization model are given. First, the equations for the NPV objective function are discussed, followed by equations for the LCAs using all the ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint indicators. This includes the two impact categories used as objective functions - GWP and FE. This is followed by an overview of the equa- tions used to calculate the CAPEX costs for each technology and asso- ciated storage units for solid and liquid products, given the seasonal nature of applying poultry manure or other fertilizers to land. Then, equations that are related to inputs from the technosphere are given, including equations for the consumption of heat, electricity, water, diesel, transportation, and energy from the CHP plant, if one is built. These equations calculating the amount of input are then translated into OPEX costs through another set of equations using the TEA parameters identified in Section S1. The OPEX cost equations also include infor- mation on disposal costs for products and fixed costs of facility man- agement. Equations for calculating the sources of revenues, including avoiding the use of inorganic fertilizer, selling products in markets, and additional policy incentives, are then presented. Finally, to constrain the use of technologies during the sensitivity analysis, additional equations were added to the model, these equations are also reported. Finally, the equations for the flow of manure through the model, from input to processing to end use are presented, one set of equations for each of the potential technologies.

In Section S4, a detailed problem formulation of the FLP problem is given. This includes the relevant assumptions for approximating the NPV objective function, the inclusion of facilities with sufficiently large supplies of manure, as well as the constraint equations for the FLP problem.
In Section S5, additional results are presented, which includes LCA, TEA, and pareto front results.

Additional LCA results include a breakdown of the causes of the environmental impacts to aid in the identification of environmental hotspots. These environmental hotspots are evaluated for the solution the maximizes NPV for each technology for Onondaga county, Jefferson county, and the average of all counties in NYS. For ALCA GWP in Fig. S1,

the hotspots for AD include the combustion of biogas in CHP and applying digestate to land. For direct land application, the hotspot is the application of poultry manure to land. For pyrolysis, HTC, and HTL, the largest impact is the combustion of solids in the CHP plant. For pyrolysis, the secondary hotspot is the combustion of syngas, while for HTL and HTC it is the disposal of GP. For ALCA FE, the hotspot for direct land application is the application of poultry manure to land, for pyrolysis, HTL, and HTC, it is combusting solids; the secondary impact for HTC and HTL is disposing of AP. For AD, the hotspot is combusting gas to CHP, followed by facility construction. For CLCA GWP hotspots in Fig. S2, for pyrolysis, it is combusting solids in the CHP plant, followed by com- busting syngas. Offsets are derived from avoided electricity and the bio- oil market. For direct land application, the largest impact is the appli- cation of poultry manure to land, though this is offset through fertilizer avoidance. For HTC and HTL, the primary hotspots are combusting solids and disposing of GP. Finally, for AD, the GWP impact is dominated by facility construction. For CLCA FE, the hotspots for pyrolysis and HTL are from combustion of solid products and bio-oil offsets. For direct land application, it is the application of poultry manure to land. For HTC and CHP, the impacts are primarily from combusting solids and disposing of the AP. For AD, the hotspots are from combusting biogas and facility construction. Furthermore, the complete LCA results on all ReCiPe 2016 midpoint indicators on different optimal solutions. In the ALCA, direct land application has the lowest impact across most ReCiPe midpoint categories, while HTL with CHP of hydrochar had the highest impact across most categories. In the CLCA, AD had the highest impact across most categories.

This is followed by a breakdown of the NPV of different counties in NYS to identify the largest sources of revenues and costs on a per-ton basis. For AD, pyrolysis, HTL, and HTC, the largest sources of costs are CAPEX and fixed OPEX costs. For direct land application, it is facility construction costs and transportation costs. Revenues for direct land application primarily come from avoided fertilizer, while for AD, py- rolysis, and HTL, revenues primarily come from bio-oil revenue and avoided electricity.

Finally, the impact of attaching carbon credits to biochar is investi- gated in the additional pareto front results subsection of Section S5. This increases the profitability of pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar. For the largest county, this changes the shape of the pareto front, removing direct land application as an optimal solution due to the large potential increases in revenue from carbon credits. For Onondaga and Jefferson county, however, the effect of carbon credits is to increase the profitability of pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar the other optimal solutions on the pareto front are unchanged.
In Section S6, references for the sources in the Supplemental Infor- mation are given.
57: In the appendix, a detailed overview of the contents of the lengthy supplemental information is given. In Section S1 of the Supplemental Information, further information on the methods used for the TEA, spatial analysis, mass balance, and LCA is included. For the TEA, the sources and parameter values for the CAPEX and OPEX costs are discussed, alongside sources and parameter values for the operational revenues of facility operation. The CAPEX cost curves are built on economies of scale models, and different parameters are derived for each type of technology. OPEX costs are broken down

into fixed costs, which include primarily plant management costs and are represented as a proportion of CAPEX costs, and variable costs, which are associated with the costs of inputs. This is followed by an overview of the economics parameters used in the sensitivity analysis, including the low, high, and nominal values of the parameters and their sources. Values in the sensitivity analysis are primarily economic in nature, although the nutrient contents of solid products are also included to model different offsets for fertilizers. Moreover, this section contains information on the spatial analysis, including the calculations to derive the supply of poultry manure on a county-level basis and the calculations to derive the average transportation distances for each county. This section also contains information on the different potential operating conditions of the different technologies of poultry manure treatment facility, which are technology dependent. For HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis, this is operating temperature, while for AD, this is organic loading rate. Product yields and product characteristics for pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, AD, and CHP under different operating conditions are also presented. Finally, this section includes the methods used to conduct the LCAs. A brief list of the different EcoInvent processes used to derive impact factors for the LCAs is given, followed by a description of the end- of-life stage of different products. The relevant impact factors for the ALCA and CLCA for all processes are then given in 25 tables.

In Section S2, the notation for the life cycle optimization model is given, including sets and indices, binary variables, non-negative continuous variables, continuous variables, and parameters. For the variables, the binary variables are the process operating conditions, the continuous variables are the NPV and LCA impacts, and the non- negative continuous variables are all other variables in the model. The nomenclature for the parameter values is presented here, the actual parameter values are scattered throughout the tables in Section S1 of the supplemental information.

In Section S3, the equations for the life cycle optimization model are given. First, the equations for the NPV objective function are discussed, followed by equations for the LCAs using all the ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint indicators. This includes the two impact categories used as objective functions - GWP and FE. This is followed by an overview of the equa- tions used to calculate the CAPEX costs for each technology and asso- ciated storage units for solid and liquid products, given the seasonal nature of applying poultry manure or other fertilizers to land. Then, equations that are related to inputs from the technosphere are given, including equations for the consumption of heat, electricity, water, diesel, transportation, and energy from the CHP plant, if one is built. These equations calculating the amount of input are then translated into OPEX costs through another set of equations using the TEA parameters identified in Section S1. The OPEX cost equations also include infor- mation on disposal costs for products and fixed costs of facility man- agement. Equations for calculating the sources of revenues, including avoiding the use of inorganic fertilizer, selling products in markets, and additional policy incentives, are then presented. Finally, to constrain the use of technologies during the sensitivity analysis, additional equations were added to the model, these equations are also reported. Finally, the equations for the flow of manure through the model, from input to processing to end use are presented, one set of equations for each of the potential technologies.

In Section S4, a detailed problem formulation of the FLP problem is given. This includes the relevant assumptions for approximating the NPV objective function, the inclusion of facilities with sufficiently large supplies of manure, as well as the constraint equations for the FLP problem.
In Section S5, additional results are presented, which includes LCA, TEA, and pareto front results.

Additional LCA results include a breakdown of the causes of the environmental impacts to aid in the identification of environmental hotspots. These environmental hotspots are evaluated for the solution the maximizes NPV for each technology for Onondaga county, Jefferson county, and the average of all counties in NYS. For ALCA GWP in Fig. S1,

the hotspots for AD include the combustion of biogas in CHP and applying digestate to land. For direct land application, the hotspot is the application of poultry manure to land. For pyrolysis, HTC, and HTL, the largest impact is the combustion of solids in the CHP plant. For pyrolysis, the secondary hotspot is the combustion of syngas, while for HTL and HTC it is the disposal of GP. For ALCA FE, the hotspot for direct land application is the application of poultry manure to land, for pyrolysis, HTL, and HTC, it is combusting solids; the secondary impact for HTC and HTL is disposing of AP. For AD, the hotspot is combusting gas to CHP, followed by facility construction. For CLCA GWP hotspots in Fig. S2, for pyrolysis, it is combusting solids in the CHP plant, followed by com- busting syngas. Offsets are derived from avoided electricity and the bio- oil market. For direct land application, the largest impact is the appli- cation of poultry manure to land, though this is offset through fertilizer avoidance. For HTC and HTL, the primary hotspots are combusting solids and disposing of GP. Finally, for AD, the GWP impact is dominated by facility construction. For CLCA FE, the hotspots for pyrolysis and HTL are from combustion of solid products and bio-oil offsets. For direct land application, it is the application of poultry manure to land. For HTC and CHP, the impacts are primarily from combusting solids and disposing of the AP. For AD, the hotspots are from combusting biogas and facility construction. Furthermore, the complete LCA results on all ReCiPe 2016 midpoint indicators on different optimal solutions. In the ALCA, direct land application has the lowest impact across most ReCiPe midpoint categories, while HTL with CHP of hydrochar had the highest impact across most categories. In the CLCA, AD had the highest impact across most categories.

This is followed by a breakdown of the NPV of different counties in NYS to identify the largest sources of revenues and costs on a per-ton basis. For AD, pyrolysis, HTL, and HTC, the largest sources of costs are CAPEX and fixed OPEX costs. For direct land application, it is facility construction costs and transportation costs. Revenues for direct land application primarily come from avoided fertilizer, while for AD, py- rolysis, and HTL, revenues primarily come from bio-oil revenue and avoided electricity.

Finally, the impact of attaching carbon credits to biochar is investi- gated in the additional pareto front results subsection of Section S5. This increases the profitability of pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar. For the largest county, this changes the shape of the pareto front, removing direct land application as an optimal solution due to the large potential increases in revenue from carbon credits. For Onondaga and Jefferson county, however, the effect of carbon credits is to increase the profitability of pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar the other optimal solutions on the pareto front are unchanged.
In Section S6, references for the sources in the Supplemental Infor- mation are given.

Equations for calculating the sources of revenues, including avoiding the use of inorganic fertilizer, selling products in markets, and additional policy incentives, are then presented. Finally, to constrain the use of technologies during the sensitivity analysis, additional equations were added to the model, these equations are also reported. Finally, the equations for the flow of manure through the model, from input to processing to end use are presented, one set of equations for each of the potential technologies.

In Section S4, a detailed problem formulation of the FLP problem is given. This includes the relevant assumptions for approximating the NPV objective function, the inclusion of facilities with sufficiently large supplies of manure, as well as the constraint equations for the FLP problem.
In Section S5, additional results are presented, which includes LCA, TEA, and pareto front results.

Additional LCA results include a breakdown of the causes of the environmental impacts to aid in the identification of environmental hotspots. These environmental hotspots are evaluated for the solution the maximizes NPV for each technology for Onondaga county, Jefferson county, and the average of all counties in NYS. For ALCA GWP in Fig. S1,

the hotspots for AD include the combustion of biogas in CHP and applying digestate to land. For direct land application, the hotspot is the application of poultry manure to land. For pyrolysis, HTC, and HTL, the largest impact is the combustion of solids in the CHP plant. For pyrolysis, the secondary hotspot is the combustion of syngas, while for HTL and HTC it is the disposal of GP. For ALCA FE, the hotspot for direct land application is the application of poultry manure to land, for pyrolysis, HTL, and HTC, it is combusting solids; the secondary impact for HTC and HTL is disposing of AP. For AD, the hotspot is combusting gas to CHP, followed by facility construction. For CLCA GWP hotspots in Fig. S2, for pyrolysis, it is combusting solids in the CHP plant, followed by com- busting syngas. Offsets are derived from avoided electricity and the bio- oil market. For direct land application, the largest impact is the appli- cation of poultry manure to land, though this is offset through fertilizer avoidance. For HTC and HTL, the primary hotspots are combusting solids and disposing of GP. Finally, for AD, the GWP impact is dominated by facility construction. For CLCA FE, the hotspots for pyrolysis and HTL are from combustion of solid products and bio-oil offsets. For direct land application, it is the application of poultry manure to land. For HTC and CHP, the impacts are primarily from combusting solids and disposing of the AP. For AD, the hotspots are from combusting biogas and facility construction. Furthermore, the complete LCA results on all ReCiPe 2016 midpoint indicators on different optimal solutions. In the ALCA, direct land application has the lowest impact across most ReCiPe midpoint categories, while HTL with CHP of hydrochar had the highest impact across most categories. In the CLCA, AD had the highest impact across most categories.

This is followed by a breakdown of the NPV of different counties in NYS to identify the largest sources of revenues and costs on a per-ton basis. For AD, pyrolysis, HTL, and HTC, the largest sources of costs are CAPEX and fixed OPEX costs. For direct land application, it is facility construction costs and transportation costs. Revenues for direct land application primarily come from avoided fertilizer, while for AD, py- rolysis, and HTL, revenues primarily come from bio-oil revenue and avoided electricity.

Finally, the impact of attaching carbon credits to biochar is investi- gated in the additional pareto front results subsection of Section S5. This increases the profitability of pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar. For the largest county, this changes the shape of the pareto front, removing direct land application as an optimal solution due to the large potential increases in revenue from carbon credits. For Onondaga and Jefferson county, however, the effect of carbon credits is to increase the profitability of pyrolysis with direct land application of biochar the other optimal solutions on the pareto front are unchanged.
In Section S6, references for the sources in the Supplemental Infor- mation are given.
62: In the appendix, a detailed overview of the contents of the lengthy supplemental information is given. In Section S1 of the Supplemental Information, further information on the methods used for the TEA, spatial analysis, mass balance, and LCA is included. For the TEA, the sources and parameter values for the CAPEX and OPEX costs are discussed, alongside sources and parameter values for the operational revenues of facility operation. The CAPEX cost curves are built on economies of scale models, and different parameters are derived for each type of technology. OPEX costs are broken down

into fixed costs, which include primarily plant management costs and are represented as a proportion of CAPEX costs, and variable costs, which are associated with the costs of inputs. This is followed by an overview of the economics parameters used in the sensitivity analysis, including the low, high, and nominal values of the parameters and their sources. Values in the sensitivity analysis are primarily economic in nature, although the nutrient contents of solid products are also included to model different offsets for fertilizers. Moreover, this section contains information on the spatial analysis, including the calculations to derive the supply of poultry manure on a county-level basis and the calculations to derive the average transportation distances for each county. This section also contains information on the different potential operating conditions of the different technologies of poultry manure treatment facility, which are technology dependent. For HTL, HTC, and pyrolysis, this is operating temperature, while for AD, this is organic loading rate. Product yields and product characteristics for pyrolysis, HTL, HTC, AD, and CHP under different operating conditions are also presented. Finally, this section includes the methods used to conduct the LCAs. A brief list of the different EcoInvent processes used to derive impact factors for the LCAs is given, followed by a description of the end- of-life stage of different products. The relevant impact factors for the ALCA and CLCA for all processes are then given in 25 tables.

In Section S2, the notation for the life cycle optimization model is given, including sets and indices, binary variables, non-negative continuous variables, continuous variables, and parameters. For the variables, the binary variables are the process operating conditions, the continuous variables are the NPV and LCA impacts, and the non- negative continuous variables are all other variables in the model. The nomenclature for the parameter values is presented here, the actual parameter values are scattered throughout the tables in Section S1 of the supplemental information.

In Section S3, the equations for the life cycle optimization model are given. First, the equations for the NPV objective function are discussed, followed by equations for the LCAs using all the ReCiPe 2016 Midpoint indicators. This includes the two impact categories used as objective functions - GWP and FE. This is followed by an overview of the equa- tions used to calculate the CAPEX costs for each technology and asso- ciated storage units for solid and liquid products, given the seasonal nature of applying poultry manure or other fertilizers to land. Then, equations that are related to inputs from the technosphere are given, including equations for the consumption of heat, electricity, water, diesel, transportation, and energy from the CHP plant, if one is built. These equations calculating the amount of input are then translated into OPEX costs through another set of equations using the TEA parameters identified in Section S1. The OPEX cost equations also include infor- mation on disposal costs for products and fixed costs of facility man- agement. Equations for calculating the sources of revenues, including avoiding the use of inorganic fertilizer, selling products in markets, and additional policy incentives, are then presented. Finally, the equations for the flow of manure through the model, from input to processing to end use are presented, one set of equations for each of the potential technologies.
